proposals.title: Capillary-Driven Breakup of Elongated Droplets in Confined Microfluidic Networks: A Pathway to Controlled Emulsification
proposals.motivation: The breakup of spherical droplets in microfluidic T-junctions and flow-focusing devices is well-characterized, providing a foundation for monodisperse emulsion generation. However, many practical systems, from biological condensates to industrial processing streams, involve non-spherical or elongated fluid threads whose breakup dynamics in confined geometries remain poorly understood. This gap limits our ability to predict and control emulsification outcomes in complex, non-ideal flows relevant to material synthesis and biomedical applications.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the breakup dynamics and final droplet size distribution for an initially elongated droplet in a confined network are governed by a non-trivial competition between the droplet's evolving shape, the local capillary number, and the confinement geometry, leading to breakup sequences distinct from those of spherical droplets or continuous jets.
proposals.method: We will employ precision microfluidic devices with well-defined geometries (e.g., symmetric/asymmetric bifurcations, sudden expansions) fabricated via soft lithography. Elongated droplets will be generated upstream. Using high-speed microscopy and particle image velocimetry (PIV), we will quantify the interfacial dynamics and flow fields. The experimental data will be compared with boundary integral simulations for Stokes flow and lattice-Boltzmann method (LBM) simulations for finite inertia, explicitly tracking the interface evolution under capillary and viscous stresses.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a predictive framework for the breakup of finite-length, non-spherical fluid entities in confined flows, directly extending the classical understanding of droplet fission. It will provide design principles for microfluidic networks that process pre-formed elongated droplets or biological condensates, with applications in advanced material templating and the analysis of biomolecular condensate transport.
proposals.novelty: While droplet generation from jets and the splitting of spherical droplets are classic problems, the systematic study of how an already-formed, finite-volume elongated droplet navigates and breaks in a confined network is novel. It bridges the gap between studies of continuous thread instability and discrete droplet dynamics.

proposals.title: Geometric Control of Bubble Breakup in Viscoelastic and Complex Fluids
proposals.motivation: The breakup of liquid droplets in microfluidic and rheometric flows is a cornerstone of soft matter physics, with well-established geometric scaling laws for pinch-off. In contrast, the breakup dynamics of gas bubbles in non-Newtonian fluids, such as polymer solutions or biofilms, is far less understood despite its relevance to industrial processes (e.g., enhanced oil recovery, fermentation) and physiological contexts (e.g., gas embolism in blood, biofilm disruption). A systematic study bridging geometric principles from droplet physics to the bubble regime is needed.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the geometric principles governing droplet pinch-off can be adapted to predict and control bubble breakup, but that critical differences will emerge due to the vanishing viscosity and density of the bubble interior, leading to distinct scaling laws and stability thresholds, especially in viscoelastic media.
proposals.method: We will employ high-speed microfluidic experiments and boundary-integral simulations. Experimentally, we will use flow-focusing and T-junction devices to generate monodisperse bubbles in aqueous polymer solutions (e.g., PEO, Xanthan gum) and model biofilm matrices (e.g., PEG-dextran). We will precisely control the capillary number and Deborah number. The breakup dynamics will be quantified via particle image velocimetry (PIV) and Lagrangian tracking of the bubble interface. The simulation framework will adapt existing boundary-integral methods for Stokes flow, treating the bubble as a constant-pressure domain, to model the viscoelastic stress response of the external fluid.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a unified geometric framework for bubble breakup, delivering predictive scaling laws for breakup time and daughter bubble size distributions in complex fluids. It will directly extend Stone group expertise in droplet dynamics and transport to the under-explored gas-liquid domain, providing fundamental insight for applications in microfluidics, materials processing, and biomedical engineering.
proposals.novelty: While droplet breakup is classic, applying geometric scaling analysis specifically to bubbles in viscoelastic fluids is novel, as the stress balance is dominated by the external fluid's rheology with negligible internal resistance, creating a distinct dynamical system. The explicit connection to biofilm-like complex fluids, a recent focus of the group, further distinguishes this work.

proposals.title: Wettability-Controlled Droplet and Particle Dynamics in Complex Microfluidic Networks
proposals.motivation: In microfluidic systems for chemical processing, diagnostics, and biomolecular condensate studies, the breakup of droplets and the adhesion of particles or cells at obstacles are critical processes. Pre-2026 work has established that flow geometry and fluid rheology govern these dynamics, but the systematic, quantitative role of surface wettability—especially its spatial patterning—remains underexplored. A predictive framework linking wettability to breakup and adhesion outcomes would enable the rational design of microreactors, filters, and lab-on-a-chip devices.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by systematically scaling and spatially patterning the wettability of channel walls and embedded obstacles, we can precisely control the critical conditions for droplet breakup and the adhesion thresholds for particles, thereby creating tunable, non-geometric flow switches and filters.
proposals.method: We will fabricate PDMS microchannels with integrated cylindrical obstacles, using plasma treatment and silane chemistry to create well-defined, spatially patterned wettability (contact angles from 30° to 150°). Dynamics of water-in-oil droplets and colloidal particle suspensions will be studied using high-speed microscopy and micro-PIV. We will quantify the modification of breakup dynamics (e.g., satellite droplet formation, necking time) and particle adhesion efficiency as functions of capillary number, obstacle arrangement, and local contact angle. Data will be analyzed to develop scaling laws that incorporate a dimensionless wettability parameter.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish quantitative design rules for using wettability as an independent control parameter in microfluidic operations, beyond traditional geometric and flow rate tuning. It will provide a fundamental understanding of how surface energy landscapes direct multiphase transport, with direct implications for the design of advanced separation devices and models for vascular adaptation where endothelial cell wettability varies.
proposals.novelty: While wettability effects are known, the systematic scaling and spatial patterning of wettability to create *predictable* and *tunable* modifications of breakup dynamics and adhesion in structured flows is a novel, non-obvious extension of current microfluidic design paradigms, which primarily focus on passive geometric control.

proposals.title: Dynamics of a core-annular flow with a deformable outer interface: transitions in stability and particle transport
proposals.motivation: Core-annular flows, where a fluid stream is perfectly confined by a coflowing immiscible fluid, are foundational models for microfluidic transport, vascular flows, and industrial processes. However, the standard assumption of a rigid or perfectly confined outer interface is often violated in biological systems (e.g., lymphatic vessels, biofilm-surrounded channels) and soft-material applications. Pre-2026 work lacks a systematic study of how finite confinement and interfacial deformability alter flow stability, solute exchange, and particle ordering, which are central to Stone's themes of flow, particles, and phase.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the finite deformability of the outer confining stream will introduce a new, tunable instability regime distinct from classic capillary or viscous fingering, and that this will fundamentally alter the axial transport and cross-stream exchange of particles and solutes compared to the perfectly confined limit.
proposals.method: We will employ a combined microfluidic and theoretical approach. Experimentally, we will use a flow-focusing device with a pressure-controlled, finite outer stream of a viscoelastic fluid (e.g., a hydrogel or polymer solution) whose interface deformation is quantified via high-speed microscopy and particle image velocimetry. Theoretically, we will develop a linear stability analysis, extending classic lubrication models to include a deformable, finite-thickness outer layer with a shear-dependent viscosity (e.g., a Carreau model). Numerical simulations using the Boundary Integral Method will model the full nonlinear interface evolution and particle transport.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new framework for predicting stability and transport in soft-confined flows, directly applicable to physiological flows and microfluidic design. It will provide quantitative criteria for the transition from confined to unconfined dynamics, linking interfacial mechanics to anomalous particle ordering and enhanced cross-stream exchange, thus bridging Stone's recent work on vascular adaptation, viscoelastic shear ordering, and condensate exchange.
proposals.novelty: While core-annular flows are well-studied, the systematic exploration of a finite, deformable outer phase as a control parameter for flow transitions and its coupling to non-Newtonian particle transport is novel. This moves beyond the standard assumption of an infinite, rigid confining wall or a simple liquid-liquid interface with fixed geometry.

proposals.title: Entrance Flow and Finite Nozzle Effects on the Stability of Viscoelastic and Particulate Jets
proposals.motivation: Classical analyses of jet stability, from Rayleigh to recent studies of complex fluids, typically assume a fully developed, parabolic velocity profile at the nozzle exit. However, in microfluidic and biological systems (e.g., vascular adaptation, biofilm streamers), nozzles are often short, and the flow is not fully developed. This discrepancy between theory and practical geometry may explain significant deviations in observed breakup lengths and instability thresholds, which remain poorly quantified.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the finite length of a nozzle and the associated entrance flow profile, characterized by a developing boundary layer and centerline acceleration, will fundamentally alter the linear stability spectrum and nonlinear breakup dynamics of jets, particularly for viscoelastic fluids and particle-laden suspensions.
proposals.method: We will combine precision microfluidic experiments with matched theoretical modeling. Experimentally, we will fabricate nozzles of systematically varied length-to-diameter ratios (L/D) and use particle image velocimetry (PIV) to map the exit velocity profile. Theoretically, we will employ a quasi-parallel stability analysis, where the base flow is the measured or computed (via finite element simulation in COMSOL) developing profile. The Orr-Sommerfeld equation will be extended for an Oldroyd-B fluid model and for a dilute suspension using an effective continuum description, with growth rates computed numerically for spatial modes.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a generalized stability criterion for jets issuing from practical, finite-length nozzles, directly applicable to microfluidic encapsulation, spray coating, and biophysical transport. It will yield predictive scaling laws linking nozzle geometry to dominant instability wavelength and breakup length, reconciling discrepancies between classic theory and experiment.
proposals.novelty: While entrance effects are well-studied in pipe flow stability, their explicit and quantitative incorporation into the free-surface jet stability problem for complex, non-Newtonian systems represents a significant and unexplored advance beyond the standard fully-developed flow assumption.

proposals.title: Interfacial Rheology in Multiphase Transport: From Biomolecular Condensates to Biofilm Dispersal
proposals.motivation: Classical fluid dynamics treats fluid-fluid interfaces as having only surface tension, neglecting their intrinsic viscoelasticity. This simplification fails for complex interfaces laden with surfactants, polymers, or biomolecules, which are ubiquitous in biological and soft matter systems. A quantitative framework for interfacial rheology is critical to understand transport in biomolecular condensates, biofilm mechanics, and the anomalous particle ordering observed in viscoelastic media.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that incorporating a finite interfacial viscosity and elasticity into multiphase flow models will fundamentally alter the predicted dynamics of droplet coalescence, breakup, and interfacial transport, providing a missing mechanistic link for diverse experimental observations.
proposals.method: We will develop a computational model using the Boundary Integral Method, augmented with the Boussinesq-Scriven constitutive law for a Newtonian interface and a linear viscoelastic (Maxwell) model. This will be implemented in an open-source framework (e.g., based on prior work with Stokesian dynamics) to simulate droplet dynamics in shear flow and pressure-driven channels. Experiments will use a microfluidic four-roll mill and a cone-and-plate interfacial rheometer to measure the deformation and relaxation of droplets with protein- or polymer-laden interfaces, directly validating the model.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a predictive, coupled bulk-interfacial rheology model for soft and biological multiphase flows. It will provide a new physical explanation for the slowed coalescence of biomolecular condensates and the modified effective viscosity of emulsions with complex interfaces, directly impacting fields from cellular biophysics to materials processing.
proposals.novelty: While interfacial rheology is studied in isolation, its dynamic coupling to bulk viscoelastic flows and its role in governing the emergent transport of embedded particles or solutes remains unexplored. This integrates Stone group expertise in anomalous particle ordering and condensate exchange into a unified interfacial framework.

proposals.title: Nonlinear Surfactant Equations of State for Interfacial Dynamics: From Droplet Breakup to Biofilm Transport
proposals.motivation: Surfactant dynamics are central to processes from droplet microfluidics to biofilm formation, yet models often assume a linear equation of state (EOS) relating interfacial tension to concentration. This simplification fails to capture the strong nonlinearities, including saturation and cooperative adsorption, observed in real systems, leading to inaccurate predictions of interfacial flows, transport, and stability. A rigorous framework for nonlinear EOS implementation is needed to bridge this gap between physicochemical reality and fluid mechanical modeling.
proposals.hypothesis: Implementing a physically realistic, nonlinear surfactant equation of state will fundamentally alter the predicted dynamics of interfacial flows, yielding new regimes of droplet deformation, breakup, and interfacial transport that are inaccessible to linear models.
proposals.method: We will implement a Frumkin or Langmuir nonlinear EOS into a boundary integral method for Stokes flow and a finite-element/volume Navier-Stokes solver (e.g., Basilisk or COMSOL). The numerical framework will couple bulk surfactant transport, nonlinear adsorption kinetics, and the dynamic interfacial tension. We will systematically test the model against classic problems: (1) the retraction and breakup of surfactant-laden filaments, (2) the Marangoni-driven motion of particles at interfaces, and (3) the stability of surfactant-laden films, comparing results directly to linear EOS predictions and available experimental data.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a validated computational toolkit for incorporating nonlinear surfactant physics into interfacial flow problems. It will establish scaling laws for flow regimes dominated by nonlinear adsorption and deliver specific, testable predictions for experiments in microfluidics and biological transport, such as the exchange dynamics of surfactant-rich condensates or biofilm interfaces.
proposals.novelty: While nonlinear adsorption isotherms are known in surface chemistry, their systematic integration into fully coupled, dynamic interfacial flow solvers to reveal new fluid mechanical phenomena remains an open challenge, moving beyond the standard linear approximation ubiquitous in pre-2026 flow studies.

proposals.title: Lattice Boltzmann Simulation of Surfactant-Mediated Droplet Dynamics in Viscoelastic Media
proposals.motivation: Surfactants critically control interfacial phenomena in multiphase flows, from emulsion stability to biofilm formation and biomolecular condensate exchange. However, simulating the coupled dynamics of fluid flow, interface deformation, and surfactant transport in complex, non-Newtonian environments remains a significant computational challenge. A predictive framework is needed to understand how viscoelastic stresses and Marangoni flows jointly dictate droplet breakup, coalescence, and transport.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the interplay between viscoelastic normal stresses and surfactant-induced Marangoni flows will lead to novel, non-intuitive droplet deformation regimes and breakup pathways that cannot be predicted by considering either effect in isolation.
proposals.method: We will develop a 3D lattice Boltzmann (LB) model coupling a Shan-Chen-type pseudopotential for multiphase flow with a FENE-CR constitutive model for viscoelasticity. Surfactant transport will be modeled via an additional distribution function for interfacial concentration, incorporating advection, diffusion, and adsorption/desorption kinetics. The Marangoni stress will be calculated from the local surfactant gradient and incorporated into the LB collision step. Simulations will be validated against microfluidic experiments with polymeric solutions and synthetic surfactants.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a unified computational tool to study surfactant-laden multiphase flows in viscoelastic systems, directly relevant to Professor Stone's interests in biofilm exchange and anomalous particle ordering. It will generate new phase diagrams for droplet stability and identify scaling laws for breakup timescales under combined elastic and capillary stresses.
proposals.novelty: While LB models exist for multiphase flow or viscoelasticity or surfactants separately, their full integration to capture three-way coupling in a single framework is novel. This approach uniquely allows the study of how bulk viscoelasticity modifies the interfacial flows driven by surfactant gradients.

proposals.title: Dynamics of Double Emulsion Formation in Ternary Fluid Systems with Controlled Partial Miscibility
proposals.motivation: Double emulsions are crucial for drug delivery, food science, and materials templating, but their formation is typically constrained to strictly immiscible fluid pairs. In biological and chemical systems, such as biomolecular condensates or multiphase reactors, fluids often exhibit partial miscibility, which fundamentally alters interfacial transport and stability. A systematic investigation of double emulsion formation under these realistic, non-ideal fluid conditions is lacking and necessary to bridge soft matter physics with applied engineering.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that controlled, finite miscibility between the three fluid streams in a microfluidic device will enable novel pathways for double emulsion formation, where dynamic solute exchange and Marangoni stresses dictate droplet morphology, stability, and encapsulation efficiency in ways not possible with immiscible systems.
proposals.method: We will employ precision microfluidics with co-flow or flow-focusing geometries, using well-characterized ternary fluid systems (e.g., water/ethanol/oil or aqueous polymer/salt/oil) where mutual solubility is tuned via temperature and composition. High-speed microscopy and micro-PIV will quantify flow fields and droplet formation dynamics. Interfacial tension and composition will be measured in situ via pendant drop tensiometry. The dynamics will be modeled using a combination of lubrication analysis for the thin film flow and phase-field simulations to capture the diffusive exchange between the partially miscible phases.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new framework for designing double emulsions with tunable permeability and stability, directly impacting controlled release technologies. It will provide fundamental insight into the coupling between fluid mechanics and phase equilibria in multiphase flows, extending concepts from biomolecular condensate exchange to synthetic fluid systems.
proposals.novelty: While Stone's group has extensively studied transport in immiscible systems and biomolecular condensates, applying these principles to the *formation* of double emulsions with intentionally leaky, miscible interfaces is a novel direction. This moves beyond the classical assumption of sharp, immiscible interfaces to explore a richer parameter space governed by inter-phase diffusion.

proposals.title: Thermochemical Control of Biomolecular Condensate Dynamics in Non-Isothermal Microfluidic Flows
proposals.motivation: The formation and exchange dynamics of biomolecular condensates, a key focus of pre-2026 biophysics, are typically studied under isothermal, equilibrium conditions. However, in vivo, these processes often occur in the presence of cellular temperature gradients (e.g., metabolic hotspots) or coupled to enzymatic reactions. Understanding how these non-isothermal or reacting conditions influence droplet nucleation, growth, and material properties is crucial for bridging in vitro studies with physiological function.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that imposing controlled temperature gradients or integrating localized chemical reactions during microfluidic droplet formation will provide a new, active mechanism to tune condensate stability, internal viscoelasticity, and selective permeability, moving beyond passive physicochemical controls.
proposals.method: We will design a microfluidic device with integrated thin-film heaters and temperature sensors to create stable, spatially defined thermal gradients across a flow-focusing junction. A second platform will incorporate surface-immobilized enzymes (e.g., kinases/phosphatases) in specific channel regions to locally modify condensate-forming proteins. Using high-speed microscopy and microparticle image velocimetry (μPIV), we will quantify the dynamics of droplet formation, coalescence, and dissolution. Fluorescence recovery after photobleaching (FRAP) and passive microrheology will be used to measure internal transport and viscoelastic moduli as functions of local temperature or reaction extent.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new experimental framework for actively manipulating biomolecular phase separation, directly linking thermal and chemical forcing to material properties. It will provide quantitative principles for how non-equilibrium conditions in cells can regulate condensate function, with potential applications in designing responsive soft materials and understanding disease-associated condensate misregulation.
proposals.novelty: While Stone's group has studied condensate exchange and particle ordering in viscoelastic flows, the explicit introduction of controlled, non-uniform temperature fields or spatially coupled chemical reactions as active control parameters during formation represents a significant departure from standard isothermal, passive approaches.

proposals.title: Inferring Flow History and Fluid Properties via the Inverse Analysis of Double Emulsion Morphology
proposals.motivation: Double emulsions, core-shell droplets with nested phases, are critical in drug delivery, food science, and materials synthesis. Their final size and structure are dictated by the complex flow conditions and fluid properties during formation, but current methods primarily predict structure from known flow parameters. An inverse method to deduce the flow history from a final droplet would be a powerful diagnostic tool for processes where in-situ flow measurement is impossible, such as in microfluidic devices with opaque components or in biological secretion pathways.
proposals.hypothesis: The final, stable morphology of a double emulsion droplet—specifically its core and shell sizes, eccentricity, and shell thickness uniformity—encodes sufficient information to inversely determine key flow parameters (e.g., capillary number, flow rate ratio, viscosity contrast) and fluid properties (e.g., interfacial tensions) that prevailed during its formation.
proposals.method: We will combine precision microfluidic experiments with a computational inverse modeling framework. Experimentally, we will generate libraries of water-oil-water double emulsions with systematically varied flow conditions and fluid properties, characterizing each final droplet via high-speed imaging. Computationally, we will train a physics-informed neural network (PINN) on data from established direct models (e.g., boundary integral simulations of two-phase breakup in confined flow). The PINN will learn the mapping from droplet morphology features (input) back to the flow and property parameters (output), regularized by the known physical constraints from lubrication theory and the Young-Laplace equation.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel inverse-analysis methodology for multiphase flows, moving beyond the standard forward-design paradigm. It will provide a non-invasive technique to 'post-mortem' diagnose flow conditions in inaccessible systems and could lead to new standards for quality control in emulsion-based manufacturing by linking final product morphology to processing history.
proposals.novelty: While forward modeling of droplet formation is mature, the explicit inversion of this problem to quantitatively back-calculate flow conditions from a single, static droplet image is largely unexplored. The approach uniquely integrates high-fidelity experimental libraries with a physics-constrained machine learning architecture tailored for ill-posed inverse problems in fluid dynamics.

proposals.title: Microfluidic Synthesis of Monodisperse Triple and Higher-Order Emulsions via Sequential Coaxial Flow Focusing
proposals.motivation: Double emulsions are valuable for drug delivery, chemical encapsulation, and as templates for microcapsules, but their utility is limited by the number of distinct compartments. Triple or higher-order emulsions, with three or more nested liquid layers, could enable unprecedented control over multi-step reactions, sequential release profiles, and the creation of complex hierarchical materials. However, robust, scalable methods for generating such monodisperse, multi-layered droplets remain a significant microfluidic challenge.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by designing a microfluidic device with a cascade of coaxial flow-focusing junctions, each stabilized by a distinct interfacial tension, we can reliably produce monodisperse triple, quadruple, and potentially higher-order emulsions in a single, continuous process.
proposals.method: We will design and fabricate glass capillary microfluidic devices featuring a sequence of nested injection capillaries. The geometry and surface wettability of each junction will be precisely controlled to stabilize the coaxial flow of three or more immiscible fluids. Using high-speed imaging and pressure-controlled pumping, we will map the parameter space (flow rate ratios, capillary numbers, fluid viscosities) to identify stable operating regimes for forming W/O/W/O or O/W/O/W triple emulsions and beyond. The stability and encapsulation efficiency will be quantified using confocal microscopy with fluorescent dyes in each phase.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a foundational platform for the deterministic fabrication of multi-layered emulsions, expanding the toolkit for soft matter and materials science. It will provide new insights into the fluid dynamics of multi-interface stability under confinement and deliver a novel class of templates for advanced encapsulation systems with programmable release kinetics.
proposals.novelty: While coaxial flow for double emulsions is well-established, the sequential, stable integration of three or more distinct fluid interfaces within a single microfluidic device is a non-trivial extension, requiring novel junction designs and a systematic understanding of the coupled hydrodynamic and interfacial constraints.

proposals.title: Viscoelastic and Shear-Thinning Effects on Droplet and Thread Dynamics in a T-Junction Microchannel
proposals.motivation: T-junction geometries are fundamental for droplet generation and fluid transport in microfluidics, but most foundational studies assume Newtonian fluids. Many biological and industrial fluids (e.g., polymer solutions, biofilms, blood) are non-Newtonian, exhibiting viscoelasticity or shear-thinning. Understanding how these complex fluid properties modify breakup dynamics, droplet size, and thread stability is crucial for designing lab-on-a-chip devices for biomedical applications and advanced materials processing.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that fluid elasticity (via polymer additives) will suppress necking instabilities and delay breakup, leading to longer threads and altered droplet sizes, while shear-thinning behavior will modify the local viscosity field during pinch-off, fundamentally changing the scaling laws governing droplet formation compared to Newtonian fluids.
proposals.method: We will use high-speed micro-PIV and confocal microscopy to visualize flow fields and interface dynamics in a PDMS T-junction. Aqueous solutions of polyacrylamide (viscoelastic) and xanthan gum (shear-thinning) at varying concentrations will be used as the dispersed phase. We will systematically vary the capillary number, flow rate ratio, and fluid relaxation time (Weissenberg number). Quantitative analysis will involve tracking the neck thinning dynamics, measuring droplet volumes, and characterizing the evolution of extensional and shear viscosity fields during breakup using particle tracking and birefringence measurements where applicable.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a comprehensive map of droplet formation regimes for canonical non-Newtonian fluids in a T-junction, providing scaling laws for droplet size as a function of rheological parameters. It will directly inform the design of microfluidic systems for handling complex biological fluids and polymer processing, bridging the gap between fundamental fluid dynamics and applied rheology.
proposals.novelty: While viscoelastic breakup in extensional flows is studied, the coupled effects of geometry-imposed shear and extension, specific to the T-junction, on non-Newtonian filament dynamics remain poorly quantified. This study uniquely dissects the interplay between fluid memory, shear-dependent viscosity, and confined geometric flow to predict novel breakup modes.

proposals.title: Surfactant Dynamics and Marangoni Stresses in the Capillary-Driven Breakup of Liquid Threads
proposals.motivation: The breakup of liquid threads and droplets is fundamental to applications from inkjet printing to drug delivery. Classical analyses assume constant interfacial tension, but real systems often contain surfactants that adsorb dynamically, creating gradients in interfacial tension (Marangoni stresses) that resist film drainage and pinch-off. A predictive, quantitative understanding of how surfactant exchange kinetics couples to flow evolution is lacking, limiting control in microfluidic and biological contexts.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the finite adsorption/desorption kinetics of soluble surfactants will introduce distinct, scalable regimes in the neck-thinning dynamics of a breaking thread, governed by a competition between surface convection, diffusion, and interfacial exchange, leading to breakup times and droplet sizes that deviate significantly from static-tension predictions.
proposals.method: We will combine precision microfluidic experiments using aqueous threads in immiscible oils with well-characterized surfactants (e.g., SDS, triblock copolymers). High-speed microscopy and interfacial particle tracking will measure thinning dynamics and surface velocity fields. These will be compared to a theoretical framework coupling the Stokes flow with a time-dependent surfactant transport equation (including bulk diffusion, adsorption/desorption, and surface convection) and a boundary integral numerical method to solve the free-surface evolution under dynamic tension.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a unified scaling framework for breakup with soluble surfactants, identifying new dimensionless groups that govern the transition between kinetics-limited and transport-limited regimes. It will provide the first direct experimental validation of Marangoni stress distributions during pinch-off, offering design principles for controlling droplet size and stability in emulsification and encapsulation processes.
proposals.novelty: While Marangoni effects are known, their quantitative, spatiotemporally resolved role during the singular process of capillary breakup, explicitly incorporating finite surfactant exchange kinetics, has not been systematically explored, moving beyond equilibrium or quasi-steady approximations.

proposals.title: Dynamics of Droplet Breakup and Coalescence in Ternary Immiscible Fluid Systems
proposals.motivation: Complex fluid systems with three or more immiscible phases are ubiquitous in nature (e.g., lipid droplets in cytoplasm) and industry (e.g., enhanced oil recovery, pharmaceutical formulations). However, classical fluid dynamics has focused predominantly on two-phase systems, leaving a significant gap in understanding the stability, breakup modes, and topological transitions in multi-component fluid mixtures. A predictive framework for these systems is critically needed.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the presence of a third immiscible fluid phase fundamentally alters the capillary and viscous stress balance during breakup and coalescence, leading to novel, non-binary droplet morphologies and breakup pathways that cannot be inferred from two-phase analogs.
proposals.method: We will employ microfluidic devices with precision flow-focusing and T-junctions to generate controlled, co-flowing streams of three immiscible fluids (e.g., water, oil, and a fluorinated oil). High-speed confocal microscopy will track interfaces and internal flows. The dynamics will be modeled using a Volume-of-Fluid (VOF) method within the open-source Basilisk flow solver, extended to handle three-phase contact lines and interfacial tension matrices, validated against experiments.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish the first systematic phase diagram for breakup regimes in ternary fluid systems, identifying new scaling laws for droplet sizes. It will provide a validated computational framework for multi-phase flow prediction, directly impacting the design of complex emulsions and informing the biophysical understanding of multi-component intracellular condensates.
proposals.novelty: While Stone's group has explored two-phase biomolecular condensate exchange and particle-laden viscoelastic flows, applying this expertise to the *simultaneous* dynamics of three fluid interfaces—where pairwise interfacial tensions and three-phase contact lines compete—is a novel and unexplored direction in soft matter physics.

proposals.title: Thermally Mediated Breakup of Droplets and Filaments: Dynamics Controlled by Spatially Varying Viscosity and Interfacial Tension
proposals.motivation: The breakup of droplets and filaments in flows is a classic problem in fluid dynamics, but most studies assume isothermal conditions. In applications from microfluidic processing to the dynamics of biomolecular condensates, temperature gradients are common and can dynamically alter local viscosity and interfacial tension, fundamentally changing the breakup process. Understanding this coupling is crucial for controlling outcomes in material synthesis and biological systems.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that imposed temperature gradients will create spatially and temporally evolving rheological properties that can either suppress or catastrophically accelerate breakup, leading to novel scaling laws and final droplet size distributions distinct from isothermal predictions.
proposals.method: We will use precision microfluidic devices with integrated thin-film heaters to impose controlled, stable temperature gradients. High-speed microscopy and micro-PIV will track flow fields and interface dynamics. Model fluids with well-characterized temperature-dependent viscosity (e.g., silicone oils) and interfacial tension (aqueous systems with surfactants) will be used. Experiments will be complemented by numerical simulations using the Volume-of-Fluid method in Basilisk, incorporating temperature-dependent material properties via user-defined functions.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new framework for predicting breakup in non-isothermal flows, providing scaling laws that incorporate the Marangoni and viscosity gradient effects. It will directly inform the design of thermal microfluidic processors and offer a new physical perspective on the stability of temperature-sensitive complex fluids and soft matter.
proposals.novelty: While thermal effects on single-phase flows are studied, their dynamic, coupled role in the finite-time singularity of capillary breakup, where both driving forces and resisting stresses evolve spatially, remains largely unexplored and non-intuitive.

proposals.title: Experimental Validation of Capillary-Driven Squeezing Dynamics and Droplet Size Scaling in Confined Microfluidic Junctions
proposals.motivation: The breakup of droplets and jets in confined geometries, such as T-junctions, is central to applications in microfluidic encapsulation, chemical synthesis, and the formation of biomolecular condensates. Recent numerical studies (e.g., by Jullien et al., 2009, and others) predict distinct scaling regimes for droplet size based on the capillary number (Ca) and the squeezing-to-dripping transition, but comprehensive experimental validation across a wide parameter space, especially for low-viscosity-ratio systems, remains incomplete. This gap limits the predictive design of microfluidic devices for generating monodisperse emulsions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the scaling law for droplet volume (V_d ~ Q_d / (Ca)^α, where α transitions from 0 to 1) in the squeezing regime is universally valid for Newtonian fluids and can be precisely mapped through controlled experiments, revealing subtle dependencies on channel geometry and fluid viscosity ratios not fully captured by 2D simulations.
proposals.method: We will fabricate PDMS microfluidic devices with well-defined T-junction geometries using soft lithography. A pressure-driven flow system (e.g., Elveflow OB1) will provide precise control over the continuous (oil) and dispersed (water/ aqueous solution) phase flow rates. High-speed microscopy will be used to visualize droplet formation. We will systematically vary the capillary number (Ca), flow rate ratio, and viscosity ratio (using water-glycerol mixtures) to span the squeezing and transition regimes. Droplet sizes will be extracted via automated image analysis (using TrackPy or a custom MATLAB algorithm), and the data will be compared to the predicted scaling laws from literature and our own boundary integral simulations for 3D effects.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a definitive experimental dataset validating the fundamental scaling laws of confined droplet breakup, directly linking numerical predictions to physical reality. It will yield a practical phase diagram for microfluidic operation and clarify the influence of 3D flow effects and viscosity contrast, which are often simplified in models. The results will be a critical reference for designing droplet-based microreactors and understanding analogous multiphase flows in porous media or biological systems.
proposals.novelty: While the squeezing regime is known, a systematic experimental study precisely mapping the scaling exponent's transition with Ca and quantifying the role of viscosity ratio in confined 3D junctions is lacking. Our approach combines high-precision microfluidics with quantitative analysis to test the numerical foundations of this canonical problem.

proposals.title: A Unified Phase Diagram for Capillary Breakup: Integrating Squeezing, Dripping, and Jetting in a Single Viscoelastic Framework
proposals.motivation: The breakup of fluid threads into droplets is a fundamental process in microfluidics, coating, and biological transport, characterized by distinct squeezing, dripping, and jetting regimes. While scaling laws exist for each regime separately, a unified theoretical model that seamlessly captures the transitions between them, especially for complex fluids, remains elusive. This gap limits predictive design in applications ranging from drug encapsulation to the dynamics of biomolecular condensates.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the transitions between squeezing, dripping, and jetting regimes can be unified within a single theoretical framework by treating the capillary number and a dimensionless viscoelastic relaxation time as the primary control parameters, with the system's response governed by a master equation that incorporates both fluid memory and interfacial stress balance.
proposals.method: We will employ a combined theoretical and experimental approach. Theoretically, we will extend a one-dimensional slender-body model for viscoelastic filaments (e.g., FENE-P or Oldroyd-B) to include confinement effects and a dynamic boundary condition at the nozzle, solved numerically using a finite-difference method. Experimentally, we will use high-speed imaging and micro-PIV to quantify the thinning dynamics and flow fields of model polymer solutions and particle-laden fluids in a microfluidic flow-focusing device, systematically varying flow rates, fluid elasticity, and confinement geometry to map the phase space.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a predictive master phase diagram for droplet generation in complex fluids, directly applicable to the design of microfluidic devices and the understanding of biological fluid fragmentation. It will establish a new scaling relationship that unifies previously disparate regimes, offering a fundamental advance in interfacial fluid dynamics.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in integrating confinement (squeezing), inertio-capillary balance (dripping), and flow focusing (jetting) into a single viscoelastic model, moving beyond Newtonian or regime-specific descriptions to create a general predictive tool for complex fluids.

proposals.title: Inverse Emulsification: Dynamics of Droplet Pinch-off from a Cross-Flowing Dispersed Phase Stream
proposals.motivation: Droplet breakup in co-flowing or cross-flowing continuous phases is foundational to emulsification and microfluidics. However, the inverse configuration, where the dispersed phase is the cross-flowing stream, is poorly understood despite its relevance to natural processes like mucus strand breakup in airway flows and industrial applications like jet-assisted mixing. This geometry introduces a novel competition between shear from the continuous phase and the intrinsic capillary instability of the dispersed jet.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the transition between dripping and jetting regimes in this inverse configuration is governed by a modified Weber number that incorporates the relative velocity vector, and that viscoelasticity in the dispersed phase will lead to asymmetric pinch-off and elongated droplet tails, distinct from classical Rayleigh-Plateau instability.
proposals.method: We will employ high-speed micro-PIV and confocal microscopy in a precision-fabricated microfluidic device with independently controlled pressure-driven flows. The dispersed phase (e.g., a Newtonian or viscoelastic polymer solution) will be injected perpendicularly into a co-flowing continuous phase stream. We will systematically vary flow rate ratios, viscosity contrasts, and fluid elasticity (using Boger fluids) to map the phase diagram. Image analysis will quantify breakup length, droplet size distribution, and pinch-off dynamics, comparing results to a modified one-dimensional slender-body model that accounts for asymmetric viscous stress.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish the first comprehensive scaling laws and regime map for droplet generation in this inverse cross-flow geometry. It will elucidate the role of fluid elasticity in asymmetric filament thinning, contributing fundamental knowledge to the dynamics of complex fluid threads in external flows. The results will provide design principles for novel microfluidic mixers and inform biophysical models of biological filament breakup.
proposals.novelty: While droplet breakup in flowing continuous phases is well-studied, systematically inverting the flow direction of the dispersed phase presents a non-obvious and unexplored fluid dynamical problem. The investigation of viscoelastic effects in this specific configuration, linking to Stone group expertise in anomalous particle ordering and biomolecular condensate exchange, is entirely novel.

proposals.title: Boundary-Induced Modifications of Run-and-Tumble Dynamics in Confined Microfluidic Flows
proposals.motivation: The transport and surface accumulation of active particles, like bacteria, in confined flows is crucial for biofilm initiation, bioremediation, and microfluidic device design. Pre-2026 models often treat swimming near boundaries as smooth, deterministic motion, ignoring the stochastic reorientations (tumbles) that are fundamental to bacterial search strategies. A rigorous understanding of how tumbles are modified by hydrodynamic and steric interactions near surfaces is lacking, creating a gap between idealized models and real microbial behavior in complex environments.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the proximity to a solid boundary fundamentally alters the statistics of the run-and-tumble process—changing tumble frequency, reorientation angle distribution, and subsequent surface escape dynamics—due to a coupling of hydrodynamic interactions, steric constraints, and potential near-surface swimming gaits.
proposals.method: We will combine microfluidic experiments with Escherichia coli strains (wild-type and smooth-swimming mutants) and quantitative tracking (e.g., µPIV/3D particle tracking velocimetry) to measure run lengths and reorientation angles near surfaces under controlled Poiseuille flow. A parallel computational approach will employ boundary element method (BEM) simulations of model swimmers (e.g., force-dipoles) with stochastic tumbling rules, explicitly incorporating hydrodynamic interactions with the no-slip boundary. The simulation framework will be validated against experimental trajectories to extract modified tumbling kernels.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first experimentally validated, mechanistic model for run-and-tumble dynamics in boundary layers, directly linking single-cell stochasticity to population-scale transport and surface accumulation. It will extend the Stone group's expertise in biofilm exchange and anomalous particle ordering to active, non-equilibrium systems, offering predictive tools for microbial ecology and microfluidic manipulation.
proposals.novelty: While run-and-tumble in bulk is well-studied, the explicit, coupled treatment of stochastic tumbling and precise near-boundary hydrodynamics is novel. This moves beyond the common assumption of reflective or random reorientation at walls, quantifying a boundary-mediated active stochastic process.

proposals.title: Hydrodynamic Trapping and Circular Swimming of Microswimmers on Chemically and Topographically Patterned Surfaces
proposals.motivation: The dynamics of active particles and microswimmers near boundaries are crucial for applications in targeted drug delivery, biofilm formation, and microfluidic sorting. Pre-2026 work has established that smooth, homogeneous walls can lead to hydrodynamic trapping and circular swimming, but real-world surfaces are chemically heterogeneous and rough. A systematic, experimental quantification of how these realistic surface properties alter near-wall hydrodynamics is lacking.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that surface roughness introduces stochastic scattering that disrupts stable circular orbits, while patterned surface chemistry creates localized variations in slip length and phoretic fields, enabling the selective trapping or guidance of specific swimmer types.
proposals.method: We will use microfluidic devices with surfaces patterned via soft lithography (for topography) and microcontact printing (for chemistry, e.g., PEG vs. hydrophobic regions). The dynamics of model microswimmers (E. coli, synthetic Janus particles) will be tracked using high-speed video microscopy and particle tracking velocimetry. The experimental data will be compared to boundary element method simulations that incorporate the measured surface geometry and prescribed slip boundary conditions to isolate hydrodynamic effects from direct contact forces.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first comprehensive map linking specific surface property scales (roughness amplitude/wavelength, chemical patch size) to alterations in swimmer trajectories and trapping efficiency. It will yield design rules for engineering surfaces in microfluidic devices and biomedical implants to control microbial adhesion and active matter transport.
proposals.novelty: While previous studies considered ideal boundaries, this work systematically decouples and combines the effects of topography and chemistry, moving beyond the perfect no-slip condition to explore how real surface imperfections and heterogeneities dictate the hydrodynamic coupling of swimmers to their environment.

proposals.title: Stokesian Dynamics of Confined Active and Passive Suspensions: Hydrodynamic Multi-Body Interactions and Emergent Transport
proposals.motivation: Understanding the collective dynamics of particles in confined, low-Reynolds-number flows is crucial for applications ranging from microfluidic transport and biofilm formation to vascular adaptation. Pre-2026, while Stokesian Dynamics (SD) is a powerful framework for modeling many-body hydrodynamic interactions in bulk, its application to complex scenarios involving walls, active particles, and viscoelastic media remains underexplored. This gap limits our ability to predict and engineer transport in biological and synthetic microsystems.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the presence of a nearby wall fundamentally alters the multi-body hydrodynamic coupling in suspensions, leading to emergent, non-additive effects on collective diffusion, self-organization, and net transport that cannot be predicted by pairwise approximations or bulk models.
proposals.method: We will develop and implement a Stokesian Dynamics simulation framework that explicitly incorporates the hydrodynamic influence of one or more plane walls using pre-2026 methods, such as Blake's image system for a Stokeslet, integrated into the SD mobility/resistance matrix formulation. The algorithm will compute many-body hydrodynamic interactions for mixtures of passive colloids and active particles (e.g., squirmers) in Newtonian and model viscoelastic fluids. Simulations will systematically vary particle concentration, activity, and wall proximity to map out phase diagrams of collective behavior.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a quantitative, first-principles simulation tool for predicting the dynamics of confined micro-suspensions, directly linking multi-body hydrodynamics to emergent transport phenomena. It will yield new design principles for microfluidic manipulation of particles and offer mechanistic insights into biological processes like nutrient exchange in biofilms or particle margination in blood flow.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in integrating a rigorous, wall-inclusive SD framework with models for active particles and non-Newtonian fluids—a combination not fully realized in pre-2026 literature—to uncover non-additive, confinement-driven collective dynamics.

proposals.title: Hydrodynamic Trapping and Transport of Self-Propelled Nanoparticles and Viruses in Microfluidic Flows
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 studies have demonstrated that micron-scale particles and cells can be hydrodynamically trapped in stagnation points, vortices, or near boundaries in microfluidic flows, influencing transport and mixing. However, it is unknown if similar trapping mechanisms govern the motion of nanoscale active particles (e.g., catalytic Janus nanoparticles) or viruses, whose dynamics are dominated by Brownian motion and low Reynolds number hydrodynamics. Understanding this is critical for applications in targeted drug delivery, viral transport in physiological flows, and the design of active nano-materials.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the combined effects of self-propulsion, Brownian motion, and background flow can lead to statistically significant, non-trivial hydrodynamic trapping and enhanced residence times for nanoparticles and viruses near geometric features or flow singularities, distinct from the deterministic trapping of larger particles.
proposals.method: We will employ microfluidic devices with precisely engineered geometries (e.g., cavities, pillars, and contraction-expansion arrays) to create controlled stagnation points and shear flows. We will track the motion of fluorescently labeled, self-propelled catalytic nanoparticles (e.g., Pt-coated Janus particles) and model viruses (e.g., bacteriophage T4) using high-speed, high-sensitivity confocal microscopy and single-particle tracking algorithms. Particle trajectories will be analyzed using statistical methods (mean-squared displacement, velocity autocorrelation) and compared to Brownian dynamics simulations that incorporate active propulsion, hydrodynamic interactions, and flow fields.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a fundamental framework for the transport of active nanoscale objects in flow, bridging the gap between micro-scale fluid mechanics and nano-scale active matter. It will provide quantitative predictions for how flow geometry can be used to manipulate and concentrate viruses or synthetic nanomotors, with direct implications for biomedical diagnostics and nano-engineering.
proposals.novelty: While hydrodynamic trapping is well-studied for passive microparticles, applying this concept to the regime where active propulsion, thermal noise, and hydrodynamics are comparable for nanoscale objects is unexplored. The investigation of viral particles in this context, linking virology to microfluidic hydrodynamics, is a novel direction.

proposals.title: Experimental Realization and Dynamics of a Toroidal Bubble in a Confined Viscous Annulus
proposals.motivation: Theoretical and computational studies in multiphase flow have long predicted the existence of stable toroidal (doughnut-shaped) bubbles under specific confinement and viscosity contrasts, but direct experimental observation remains elusive. Validating this structure is crucial for advancing fundamental understanding of complex fluid interfaces and has implications for processes ranging from microfluidic encapsulation to volcanic degassing, where such non-spherical geometries may govern transport and stability.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by injecting air into a highly viscous, immiscible liquid annulus confined within a co-flowing aqueous stream, a stable toroidal bubble can be formed and its structure, stability, and breakup dynamics can be systematically characterized.
proposals.method: We will employ a precision coaxial microfluidic device to create a core-annulus flow, where a viscous oil (e.g., silicone oil or a glycerol-water mixture) forms the annulus surrounding a central aqueous stream. A controlled air injection through a side port into the annulus will initiate bubble formation. High-speed micro-Particle Image Velocimetry (μ-PIV) with fluorescent tracer particles in the outer fluid and direct shadowgraphy will be used to visualize the three-dimensional bubble shape and the surrounding flow field. The experiment will systematically vary the capillary number (via flow rates and viscosity) and the confinement ratio (annulus thickness) to map the parameter space for toroidal stability.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first direct experimental evidence and quantitative characterization of a toroidal bubble in a viscous annulus, establishing a benchmark for theory. It will yield new insights into the stability criteria and breakup modes of complex interfacial structures in confined multiphase flows, directly contributing to the physics of soft matter and transport phenomena.
proposals.novelty: While toroidal droplets and bubbles have been studied in pure fluids or via numerical simulation, the experimental creation and analysis of a toroidal bubble *within* a confined viscous fluid layer, explicitly testing the coupling between interfacial tension, viscous stresses, and confinement, is a novel and non-trivial fluid mechanics challenge.

proposals.title: Lattice Boltzmann Simulation of Coalescence Dynamics in Viscosity-Mismatched Droplets and Biomolecular Condensates
proposals.motivation: The coalescence of droplets with mismatched viscosities is a fundamental process in applications ranging from microfluidic encapsulation to the maturation of biomolecular condensates in cells. While the coalescence of identical Newtonian droplets is well-studied, the dynamics for droplets of different viscosities, especially in the presence of complex interfacial rheology, remain poorly understood. This gap limits our ability to predict and control phase evolution in soft materials and biological systems.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that viscosity contrast between coalescing droplets introduces a novel, asymmetric flow field that fundamentally alters the neck growth dynamics and internal mixing patterns, deviating from classical scaling laws. Furthermore, we posit that interfacial viscoelasticity, relevant to biomolecular condensates, can either arrest or guide this process.
proposals.method: We will employ a multi-component, multi-relaxation-time (MRT) lattice Boltzmann method (LBM) with a Shan-Chen-type pseudopotential model to simulate two-phase flow with tunable viscosity and density ratios. The code will be implemented on GPU-accelerated architectures (e.g., CUDA) to enable high-resolution 3D simulations. We will systematically vary viscosity ratios (0.01 to 100) and incorporate a Maxwell viscoelastic constitutive model at the interface to study the effects of interfacial rheology on coalescence dynamics.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first comprehensive phase diagram for the coalescence of viscosity-mismatched droplets, quantifying the transition in neck growth scaling from viscous to inertia-dominated regimes. It will establish a predictive computational framework for multi-fluid systems with complex interfaces, directly informing our understanding of transport and maturation in biomolecular condensates and industrial emulsions.
proposals.novelty: While LBM has been used for two-phase flow, its application to systematically study the full, asymmetric coalescence problem with large viscosity contrasts and explicit interfacial viscoelasticity is novel. This bridges Stone group expertise in complex fluid transport with advanced mesoscale simulation, moving beyond prior studies focused on symmetric or Stokes flow limits.

proposals.title: Early-time dynamics of nanoscale drop coalescence: bridging molecular interactions and continuum hydrodynamics
proposals.motivation: The coalescence of microscopic and nanoscopic liquid drops is a fundamental process in applications ranging from inkjet printing and emulsion stability to the fusion of biomolecular condensates in cellular biology. While macroscale coalescence is well-described by viscous and inertial dynamics, the early-time regime for sub-micron drops remains poorly understood, as molecular-scale interactions and thermal fluctuations may dominate over continuum hydrodynamic forces. Understanding this crossover is critical for controlling material and biological systems where small-scale liquid-liquid interfaces are prevalent.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that for nanoscale drops, the initial bridge growth during coalescence transitions from a thermally activated, stochastic process at the smallest scales to a deterministic, viscosity-dominated regime described by a modified Stokes-flow model, with the crossover length scale set by the ratio of thermal energy to interfacial tension.
proposals.method: We will employ high-speed interferometry and fluorescence microscopy to track the coalescence of monodisperse, surfactant-free emulsion droplets (50 nm to 5 μm radius) generated via microfluidics. For the smallest drops, we will use molecular dynamics (MD) simulations with a coarse-grained water model to capture thermal effects. The experimental and simulation data will be analyzed using a stochastic differential equation framework to model bridge evolution, explicitly incorporating thermal noise and a disjoining pressure derived from measured van der Waals and hydration forces.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a unified theoretical and experimental framework for drop coalescence across scales, quantitatively predicting the bridge growth law from the molecular to the continuum regime. It will provide the first direct measurements of the thermal-to-hydrodynamic crossover, offering predictive design rules for nanoemulsions and a physical basis for understanding the rapid fusion dynamics of intracellular condensates.
proposals.novelty: While prior studies have examined coalescence in either the purely continuum or purely molecular limit, this work uniquely focuses on the unexplored crossover regime where both thermal fluctuations and hydrodynamic dissipation are significant, directly connecting molecular-scale interactions to emergent liquid-phase behavior.

proposals.title: Collective Dynamics and Flow Topology in the Simultaneous Coalescence of Three or More Liquid Droplets
proposals.motivation: The coalescence of two droplets is a canonical fluid dynamics problem, foundational to understanding processes from raindrop formation to inkjet printing. However, many natural and industrial systems involve the simultaneous contact and merger of three or more droplets, a regime where complex multi-body hydrodynamic and capillary interactions are expected but remain unexplored. This gap limits our predictive understanding of phenomena like spray coating, phase separation in condensates, and the sintering of particulate suspensions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the simultaneous coalescence of N≥3 droplets creates unique, transient flow topologies and internal mixing patterns not present in binary coalescence, governed by a competition between capillary-driven bridge growth and viscous dissipation that depends critically on the initial droplet configuration (e.g., linear chains vs. triangular clusters).
proposals.method: We will employ high-speed interferometry and particle image velocimetry (PIV) to track the evolving interface shapes and internal flow fields within coalescing droplet clusters. Experiments will use silicone oil and water-glycerol mixtures in an immersion oil bath to vary viscosity and surface tension. A custom micro-positioning stage will bring three or more droplets into controlled, simultaneous contact. Complementary boundary integral simulations, validated against experiments, will map the parameter space (Ohnesorge number, contact geometry) and elucidate the stress fields driving the coalescence dynamics.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish the first experimental and theoretical framework for multi-droplet coalescence, quantifying how collective interactions alter merger timescales and internal mixing efficiency. It will provide predictive scaling laws for the evolution of the connected fluid network, directly informing models for sintering, emulsion stability, and the ripening of biomolecular condensates.
proposals.novelty: While two-drop coalescence is well-characterized, the dynamics of three or more droplets coalescing simultaneously is an uncharted problem where non-linear interactions and geometric constraints are predicted to lead to emergent flow structures, such as competing liquid bridges and vortex rings, not seen in pairwise mergers.

proposals.title: Reciprocal Coupling of Microswimmer Locomotion and Non-Newtonian Fluid Rheology
proposals.motivation: Microswimmers in biological and engineered systems often navigate complex, non-Newtonian fluids like mucus or polymer solutions. While the impact of fluid rheology on swimmer propulsion is studied, the reciprocal effect—where the swimmer's flow field locally modifies the fluid's microstructure and viscoelastic properties, creating a dynamic feedback loop—remains poorly understood. This two-way coupling is crucial for accurately predicting transport in biological contexts like bacterial infection or targeted drug delivery.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a microswimmer's flow field can induce significant local alterations in the microstructure and rheological state of a viscoelastic fluid, and that this modified environment feeds back to substantially alter swimming kinematics and efficiency in a manner predictable from measurable material properties.
proposals.method: We will employ a combined experimental and theoretical approach. Experimentally, we will use well-characterized model swimmers (e.g., magnetic helices, active colloids) in transparent viscoelastic fluids (e.g., Boger fluids, polymeric solutions). Particle Image Velocimetry (PIV) and birefringence imaging will quantify flow fields and local polymer alignment/stretch. Theoretically, we will develop a two-way coupled model using a continuum description (Oldroyd-B or Giesekus constitutive equation) solved numerically via an immersed boundary method, where the swimmer's prescribed surface motion drives the flow, which in turn evolves the fluid stress field that modifies the swimmer's motion.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a foundational framework for two-way swimmer-medium rheology coupling, moving beyond the standard one-way assumption. It will yield predictive scaling laws linking swimmer kinematics to fluid properties and activity, and provide high-fidelity experimental data for validation. This directly advances understanding of microbial transport in bodily fluids and the design of microrobots for complex environments.
proposals.novelty: While prior work treats the fluid as a static background, our proposal explicitly focuses on the dynamic, spatiotemporally evolving rheological state *caused by* the swimmer, and its mechanical feedback. This integrates Stone group expertise in anomalous particle ordering under flow and transport in adaptive systems into the active matter realm.

proposals.title: Minimalist Active Fluids: Emergent Transport and Phase Behavior in a Model System of Light-Activated Colloids
proposals.motivation: Active suspensions, from bacterial swarms to cytoskeletal networks, exhibit complex collective phenomena like swarming, turbulence, and phase separation that challenge theoretical models. However, existing experimental systems are often biologically complex or synthetically intricate, obscuring the fundamental physical principles. A conceptually simple, tunable model system is needed to provide clean data to guide and test theoretical frameworks for active matter.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a minimal experimental system of photo-activated colloidal particles in a Newtonian fluid will exhibit a sequence of distinct, predictable collective states—from enhanced diffusion to vortex formation and macroscopic flow—governed by a small set of dimensionless parameters related to activity, concentration, and confinement.
proposals.method: We will fabricate uniform silica colloids half-coated with a light-absorbing carbon layer. Suspended in water, these 'Janus' particles become self-propelled under uniform visible light illumination due to self-thermophoresis. Using high-speed video microscopy and particle tracking in well-defined microfluidic geometries (straight channels, closed chambers), we will systematically vary the light intensity (controlling propulsion speed), particle volume fraction, and boundary conditions. The dynamics will be quantified via metrics like the effective diffusivity, velocity correlation functions, and vorticity fields, directly comparing results to continuum active nematics and agent-based simulation predictions.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a benchmark experimental platform for active matter physics, providing unambiguous data on how macroscopic transport and coherent structures emerge from microscopic activity. It will directly test the validity of current continuum theories for active suspensions and reveal the role of boundaries in shaping collective flow patterns, with implications for understanding biofilm initiation and targeted drug delivery.
proposals.novelty: While active colloids are studied, our approach uniquely emphasizes extreme simplification and parameter isolation to serve as a direct experimental testbed for theory, contrasting with studies focused on biological realism or engineering complex synthetic swimmers.

proposals.title: Chaotic Mixing and Transport in Viscoelastic and Shear-Thinning Fluids: A Lagrangian Analysis
proposals.motivation: Efficient mixing of non-Newtonian fluids is critical in bioprocessing (e.g., polymeric solutions, biofilms) and materials synthesis, yet the fundamental interplay between chaotic advection and complex rheology remains poorly understood. Pre-2026 studies of chaotic mixing are largely confined to Newtonian fluids, creating a significant gap for predicting transport in industrially and biologically relevant complex fluids where elasticity and shear-dependent viscosity dominate.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that fluid elasticity and shear-thinning behavior will fundamentally alter Lagrangian coherent structures and mixing efficiency in a chaotic flow, leading to the emergence of anomalous transport regimes and persistent concentration heterogeneities not observed in Newtonian systems.
proposals.method: We will employ a well-characterized, two-dimensional chaotic mixer (e.g., a journal bearing flow or a time-periodic lid-driven cavity) and introduce model viscoelastic (Boger fluids) and shear-thinning (xanthan gum solutions) fluids. Using micro-particle image velocimetry (μ-PIV) and particle tracking velocimetry (PTV) with fluorescent tracer particles, we will quantify the flow field and Lagrangian particle trajectories. Finite-time Lyapunov exponent (FTLE) fields will be computed from the experimental velocity data to identify coherent structures, while mixing efficiency will be quantified via the intensity of segregation from planar laser-induced fluorescence (PLIF) of a passive scalar.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish the first experimental Lagrangian framework for chaotic mixing in canonical non-Newtonian fluids, directly linking rheological parameters to transport topology and mixing rates. It will provide predictive design principles for mixers handling complex fluids and offer new insights into transport limitations in biological systems like polymeric mucus or extracellular matrices.
proposals.novelty: While chaotic mixing is well-studied, applying Lagrangian dynamical systems analysis—specifically FTLE fields and coherent structure identification—to experimentally measured flows of viscoelastic and shear-thinning fluids is a novel synthesis of Stone group expertise in complex fluid transport and flow analysis.

proposals.title: Homogenization of Composite Slip Boundaries: Predicting Effective Slip Lengths for Surfaces with Distributed Microbubbles
proposals.motivation: Superhydrophobic and bubble-laden surfaces promise significant drag reduction in microfluidic and marine applications by introducing local slip. However, predicting the effective slip for a surface with a complex, heterogeneous distribution of slip regions remains a challenge, often requiring computationally intensive direct numerical simulations. A predictive, averaged framework is needed to connect microscopic interfacial physics to macroscopic flow properties for rational surface design.
proposals.hypothesis: The effective slip length of a surface patterned with nearly flat microbubbles can be accurately modeled using composite material homogenization techniques, where the surface is treated as a two-dimensional composite of no-slip (solid) and finite-slip (air-water interface) regions.
proposals.method: We will adapt the Mori-Tanaka effective medium theory, classically used for conductivity of particulate composites, to the hydrodynamic slip problem. The solid matrix is assigned a zero local slip length, while oblate spheroidal inclusions (bubbles) are assigned a finite intrinsic slip length, informed by prior molecular dynamics and experimental results. The key analytical step is solving the associated boundary value problem for flow over a single inclusion to derive its polarizability, which is then averaged over the surface coverage and inclusion geometry. The model will be validated against finite-element simulations of Stokes flow over discrete bubble arrays.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a closed-form, predictive model for effective slip, directly linking bubble geometry, surface coverage, and intrinsic interface physics to a measurable macroscopic flow property. It will establish a rigorous theoretical bridge between composite materials science and hydrodynamic slip, offering a practical design tool for engineered slip surfaces.
proposals.novelty: While effective medium theories are standard in materials science, their direct application to homogenize hydrodynamic boundary conditions on a surface, treating local slip lengths as a material property, is a novel conceptual and analytical leap beyond existing numerical or perturbation-based approaches for superhydrophobic surfaces.

proposals.title: Shear-Dependent Effective Slip from Dynamically Deforming Bubble Carpets: A Lattice Boltzmann Simulation and Constitutive Modeling Study
proposals.motivation: Superhydrophobic and lubricant-infused surfaces promise dramatic drag reduction via effective slip at the liquid-gas interface of surface microstructures. However, pre-2026 models often assume static bubble shapes and a constant effective slip length, which fails under practical flow conditions where shear stresses dynamically deform and rearrange the interfacial features. A predictive, mechanistic understanding of this shear-dependent slip is crucial for designing robust, tunable surfaces for applications in microfluidics, marine coatings, and vascular flow systems.
proposals.hypothesis: The effective slip length of a bubble carpet is a non-linear, shear-dependent property governed by the dynamic deformation and collective rearrangement of individual bubbles, which can be captured by a constitutive model parameterized from high-resolution flow simulations.
proposals.method: We will employ a two-phase, immiscible lattice Boltzmann method (LBM) with a Shan-Chen-type pseudopotential to simulate shear flow over a substrate patterned with gas-filled cavities. The LBM will resolve the dynamic deformation of individual bubble menisci and the surrounding flow field. Simulation outputs—local shear rates, meniscus curvature, and local slip—will be used to inform and validate a theoretical framework that upscales these effects into a constitutive relation for effective slip length as a function of global shear rate and surface geometry.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first closed-loop, simulation-informed constitutive model for shear-dependent effective slip, moving beyond static approximations. It will deliver a predictive design tool for optimizing surface textures in dynamic flow environments and offer fundamental insights into the coupling between interfacial mechanics and transport, relevant to biofilm-laden flows and vascular adaptation.
proposals.novelty: The explicit, two-way coupling of high-resolution LBM simulations of dynamic bubble deformation with a constitutive slip model is novel, enabling the prediction of non-linear slip behavior from first-principles bubble mechanics rather than phenomenological fitting.

proposals.title: Effective Medium Theory for Macroscopic Slip: A Perturbative Framework for Sparse Bubble and Superhydrophobic Surfaces
proposals.motivation: Predicting drag reduction from superhydrophobic surfaces or sparse bubble arrays is crucial for energy-efficient transport in microfluidics and marine applications. Current models often rely on specific, idealized surface geometries or numerical simulations, lacking a general analytical link between sparse surface microstructure and the effective macroscopic slip length. A rigorous, predictive theory for low surface coverage remains an open challenge in fluid dynamics.
proposals.hypothesis: The effective slip length for a wall sparsely populated with no-shear regions (bubbles) can be derived as a first-order correction to the classic no-slip flow using a perturbation expansion in the small parameter of surface coverage, analogous to the treatment of dilute inclusions in electromagnetic effective medium theory.
proposals.method: We will treat the no-slip flow as the unperturbed (zeroth-order) state. The sparse, no-shear bubbles will be modeled as a perturbation to the boundary condition, with the area fraction as the small expansion parameter. Using a Green's function approach for the Stokes equations, we will calculate the first-order correction to the flow field and integrate to obtain an analytical expression for the effective slip length.
