This framework will be validated against existing lattice Boltzmann and boundary integral simulations for patterned surfaces.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a closed-form, predictive expression linking bubble size, distribution, and coverage to macroscopic slip, directly quantifying drag reduction. It will establish a new analytical framework for sparse surface heterogeneities, bridging composite material theory and interfacial fluid mechanics.
proposals.novelty: The approach is novel in directly adapting the mathematical structure of perturbation methods from electromagnetic effective medium theory to a low-Reynolds-number hydrodynamic boundary condition problem, providing a rigorous asymptotic solution for a long-standing problem in slip flow.

proposals.title: A Perturbation Framework for Flow Over Surfaces with Sparse No-Shear Bubbles: Implications for Biofilm Transport and Gas Exchange
proposals.motivation: Surfaces with mixed boundary conditions, such as a no-slip wall decorated by a sparse distribution of no-shear bubbles (e.g., plastrons on superhydrophobic surfaces or gas-filled pores in biofilms), are critical for drag reduction, gas exchange, and nutrient transport. However, predicting flow and transport for low, non-uniform surface gas coverage remains a challenge, as existing models often assume uniform slip or high coverage fractions. A systematic low-coverage theory is needed to bridge the gap between idealized models and realistic, heterogeneous interfaces prevalent in biological and engineered systems.
proposals.hypothesis: For a surface with a low area fraction of no-shear regions, the leading-order correction to the flow field and associated scalar transport (e.g., oxygen flux) can be captured via a regular perturbation expansion, with the small parameter being the surface gas coverage, and that this correction will be non-local and dependent on bubble arrangement.
proposals.method: We will employ a dual theoretical and experimental approach. Theoretically, we will develop a perturbation expansion in the small surface coverage parameter, treating isolated no-shear bubbles as boundary condition defects on a no-slip plane. The leading-order flow field will be solved using a boundary integral method or a reciprocal theorem approach, mapping the problem to the disturbance created by point stresslets. Experimentally, we will use micro-particle image velocimetry (micro-PIV) to measure flow fields over microfabricated surfaces with precisely controlled, sparse bubble arrays in a microfluidic channel, validating the perturbation predictions.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a foundational analytical framework for quantifying flow and transport over sparsely bubbly surfaces, directly applicable to models of gas exchange in heterogeneous biofilms and the performance of superhydrophobic surfaces at low gas fractions. It will yield a predictive scaling law for effective slip length and mass transfer coefficient as a function of bubble coverage and spatial distribution.
proposals.novelty: While perturbation methods are standard in fluid mechanics, their application to systematically derive flow and transport laws for randomly distributed, finite-sized no-shear defects on a wall at low coverage is novel, moving beyond area-averaged effective boundary conditions to capture arrangement-dependent effects.

proposals.title: Engineering a Polydisperse Bubble Layer for Robust Turbulent Drag Reduction in Pipe Flow
proposals.motivation: Drag reduction in pipelines offers immense energy savings for fluid transport industries. While microbubble injection is known to reduce skin friction, its application is limited by the need for precise, energy-intensive control of monodisperse bubbles and their poor stability in turbulent flows. A scalable, retrofittable method that creates a stable, effective slip layer without permanent surface modification remains a critical unsolved challenge.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a deliberately engineered, polydisperse distribution of microbubbles, generated and sustained by controlled flow instabilities at the pipe wall, will create a robust, adaptive effective slip boundary, leading to significant and sustained drag reduction in turbulent pipe flow.
proposals.method: We will use a custom-built, recirculating turbulent pipe flow facility with optical access. Bubble generation will be achieved via chaotic advection using passive, 3D-printed inlets and active pulsations to create a tailored polydisperse size distribution. High-speed particle image velocimetry (PIV) and shadowgraphy will simultaneously measure the near-wall velocity field and bubble layer dynamics. Drag will be quantified via pressure drop measurements. The system will be systematically varied to map the relationship between inlet geometry, pulsation frequency, resultant bubble size distribution, layer stability, and drag reduction efficiency.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new framework for designing dynamic, bubble-based slip boundaries, moving beyond idealized monodisperse systems. It will provide quantitative, mechanistic insight into how polydispersity enhances layer stability and drag reduction. The findings will directly inform the design of low-energy, retrofittable systems for industrial fluid transport.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in exploiting, rather than suppressing, flow instabilities to *generate* the functional polydisperse bubble layer, and in demonstrating that a tailored size distribution is key to robustness, contrasting with prior focus on monodisperse or uncontrolled bubbly flows.

proposals.title: Geometric Control of Bubble Pinch-off and Transport in Non-Circular Microchannels
proposals.motivation: The dynamics of bubble formation and pinch-off in microfluidic channels are central to applications in materials synthesis, chemical processing, and biomedical flows. While extensive work has characterized these processes in cylindrical capillaries, the role of channel cross-sectional shape—a critical design parameter in modern soft lithography—remains underexplored. Understanding how rectangular or triangular geometries alter interfacial curvature and flow confinement could unlock new strategies for controlling dispersed phase systems.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the sharp corners and flat walls of non-circular channels will impose anisotropic capillary pressures and viscous stresses, fundamentally altering the bubble neck thinning dynamics, final pinch-off location, and the resulting bubble shape and stability compared to circular channels.
proposals.method: We will fabricate PDMS microfluidic devices with circular, rectangular (varying aspect ratio), and triangular cross-sections. Using high-speed microscopy and particle image velocimetry (PIV), we will study air bubble generation in a continuous water-glycerol phase. The dynamics will be quantified by measuring the neck radius evolution, pinch-off time, and bubble velocity. Experiments will be complemented by numerical simulations using the Volume-of-Fluid (VOF) method in a finite-element framework (e.g., COMSOL) to resolve the three-dimensional interface evolution and corner flow fields.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a framework for predicting bubble formation in arbitrary channel geometries, linking device design to flow outcomes. It will provide new fundamental insights into how confinement shape influences multiphase flow stability, directly extending the Stone group's expertise in transport and phase dynamics to geometrically complex systems.
proposals.novelty: Previous studies on bubble pinch-off in confinement largely assume axisymmetric (circular) geometries. Systematically probing the non-axisymmetric, corner-dominated flow regimes in rectangular and triangular channels represents a significant and non-obvious departure from this canonical view.

proposals.title: Experimental and Theoretical Mapping of the Inertial Regime in Bubbly Flows: Control Limits for Size and Fraction at Finite Reynolds Numbers
proposals.motivation: In industrial processes like flotation and chemical reactors, precise control over bubble size and volume fraction is critical for efficiency and yield. While the Stokes regime is well-understood, most practical applications operate at finite Reynolds numbers where inertial effects—wake interactions, modified drag, and deformation—fundamentally alter the flow physics and control possibilities. A systematic mapping of the experimentally accessible parameter space in this inertial regime is lacking, hindering the optimization of high-throughput multiphase systems.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that finite Reynolds number effects impose new, quantifiable constraints on the independent control of bubble size and volume fraction, leading to a phase diagram with distinct regions of stable, coupled, and unstable control defined by Re, We, and void fraction.
proposals.method: We will develop a microfluidic flow-focusing platform with integrated pressure and flow sensors to independently vary gas pressure and liquid flow rates, generating monodisperse bubbles across a wide range of Re (1-500) and We (0.01-10). High-speed imaging and particle image velocimetry will quantify bubble dynamics and wake structures. This experimental data will be used to inform and validate a generalized theoretical model that extends the Stokes-based control framework by incorporating inertial corrections for drag and added mass, and a criterion for wake-mediated bubble-bubble interactions.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first comprehensive 'phase diagram' for bubbly flows in the inertial regime, delineating the limits of independent control. It will provide a predictive framework for engineers to select stable operating conditions in industrial reactors and advance fundamental understanding of multiphase hydrodynamics beyond creeping flow.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in fusing precise multiphase microfluidics—typically used for low-Re studies—with systematic inertial hydrodynamics to map the complete control parameter space, moving beyond the established creeping-flow limits to a regime where bubble interactions are dynamically mediated by fluid inertia.

proposals.title: Direct Measurement of the Singular Stress and Pressure Fields Governing Bubble Pinch-Off
proposals.motivation: The pinch-off of a bubble is a classic example of a finite-time singularity in fluid mechanics, yet a complete mechanical description remains elusive. Pre-2026 studies have characterized neck geometry and scaling laws, but the coupled internal dynamics—the interplay of viscous stress, inertia, and local pressure gradients—have not been measured simultaneously. This gap limits predictive models for processes involving bubble rupture, such as aerosol generation, sonochemistry, and gas exchange in bioreactors.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the singularity formation is governed by a dynamic balance where the local pressure minimum at the neck, measured directly, dictates the acceleration of the internal flow field, and that deviations from inertial-viscous scaling in complex fluids can be quantitatively linked to measured viscoelastic normal stresses.
proposals.method: We will develop a synchronized multi-modal system: (1) Ultra-high-speed video microscopy (1M fps) to track the external neck radius R(t), (2) Micro-PIV using 200 nm fluorescent tracers and a dual-cavity Nd:YLF laser to resolve the 2D velocity field u(r,z,t) within the neck, and (3) A micro-fabricated, piezoresistive pressure sensor (~50 kHz bandwidth, <10 µm tip) integrated into the nozzle to record p(t). Custom algorithms will perform spatiotemporal correlation of PIV data with the neck boundary and synchronize it with pressure traces to compute full stress fields (σ = -pI + 2μE, where E is the strain-rate tensor).
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first experimentally closed momentum balance for a pinching bubble, directly validating or challenging existing singularity theories. It will establish a new benchmark dataset for multi-phase flow simulations and provide a mechanistic framework for understanding pinch-off in non-Newtonian and surfactant-laden systems, directly relevant to Professor Stone's interests in complex fluid transport and biofilm-related exchange processes.
proposals.novelty: The non-obvious integration of a microscale, high-frequency pressure sensor directly into the pinch-off region, synchronized with μ-PIV, allows for the first direct experimental calculation of the complete stress tensor during singularity formation, moving beyond indirect inference from shape analysis alone.

proposals.title: Direct Measurement of Scalar Transport During Bubble Pinch-Off via Planar Laser-Induced Fluorescence
proposals.motivation: The pinch-off of a bubble is a fundamental fluid dynamics event involving extreme interfacial curvature and rapid flow, yet the associated mass transfer and mixing processes remain poorly characterized. Understanding these processes is critical for applications ranging from chemical reactors and aeration to biomedical gas exchange and volcanic degassing. Current knowledge relies on structural imaging or bulk measurements, lacking direct, time-resolved data on scalar fields like dissolved gas concentration in the critical neck region.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the rapid pinch-off of a bubble generates a transient, localized scalar field with strong gradients and potentially anomalous transport, driven by a combination of capillary-driven flow and viscous stresses, which can be directly quantified using laser-induced fluorescence.
proposals.method: We will adapt planar laser-induced fluorescence (PLIF), a combustion diagnostic, to a controlled bubble pinch-off experiment. A high-speed, pulsed laser sheet will illuminate the neck region of a bubble pinching from a submerged orifice. The liquid will be seeded with a fluorescent dye (e.g., Rhodamine 6G) whose intensity, after careful calibration, correlates with the concentration of a dissolved scalar (e.g., oxygen, using a quenching-sensitive dye, or a passive chemical tracer). A high-speed camera, synchronized to the laser pulses, will capture the 2D fluorescence field at rates exceeding 50,000 fps. Image processing will involve background subtraction, intensity calibration against known concentrations, and computation of scalar gradients and fluxes.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first direct, quantitative maps of scalar concentration evolution during bubble pinch-off, revealing the mechanisms of entrainment, stretching, and diffusive transport. It will establish a new experimental paradigm for studying interfacial mass transfer in unsteady, high-curvature flows, directly complementing Professor Stone's work on exchange dynamics in biofilms and vascular systems.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the direct adaptation of a high-speed, laser-based scalar field mapping technique from combustion to the classic bubble pinch-off problem, enabling visualization of a previously inaccessible dimension—the dissolved species concentration—during this ultra-fast event.

proposals.title: High-Speed Imaging of Bubble Pinch-Off in Complex Fluids: Elucidating the Role of Viscoelasticity and Interfacial Transport
proposals.motivation: The pinch-off of bubbles is a fundamental fluid dynamics process relevant to industrial foaming, bioreactor design, and geophysical flows. While well-studied in Newtonian fluids, the dynamics in complex, viscoelastic fluids—where elastic stresses and transport of surfactants or particles to the interface can dominate—remain poorly understood. A detailed mechanistic study is needed to bridge the gap between classical pinch-off scaling and the anomalous dynamics observed in structured fluids.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the finite extensibility of polymer molecules in a viscoelastic solution and the dynamic adsorption of interfacial-active species (e.g., from a surrounding biofilm or condensate phase) will fundamentally alter the neck thinning dynamics and satellite formation during bubble pinch-off, leading to distinct scaling regimes not captured by inertial-viscous-capillary balances.
proposals.method: We will employ ultra-high-speed video microscopy (up to 1,000,000 fps) coupled with microfluidic flow-focusing devices to generate and control bubble formation in aqueous solutions of flexible polymers (e.g., PEO) and particle-laden fluids. Custom particle image velocimetry (PIV) and digital image analysis algorithms will be used to track the interface shape, neck radius R(t), and flow fields. Experiments will systematically vary fluid relaxation time, extensional viscosity (via CaBER pre-characterization), and interfacial tension dynamics using fluorescent surfactants to quantify adsorption kinetics during pinch-off.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first temporally resolved, quantitative dataset for bubble pinch-off in biologically and industrially relevant complex fluids. It will establish new scaling laws that incorporate fluid memory and interfacial transport, directly contributing to models for multiphase flows in polymer processing and biomedical systems where gas exchange occurs in non-Newtonian environments.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in applying high-resolution, high-speed diagnostics—common in Newtonian fluid dynamics—to dissect the coupled roles of bulk viscoelasticity and dynamic surface activity, a synthesis inspired by Stone group work on anomalous particle ordering in viscoelastic flow and biomolecular condensate exchange.

proposals.title: Real-Time Control of Effective Slip in Microstructured Flows via Homogenized Models and Optical Feedback
proposals.motivation: Microstructured surfaces, such as superhydrophobic or liquid-infused patterns, offer precise control over effective slip, crucial for applications like drag reduction and particle focusing. However, current designs are static and cannot adapt to changing flow conditions or performance degradation. A closed-loop system that dynamically tunes microscale boundary conditions to maintain a target macroscopic flow property would enable robust, adaptive microfluidic devices.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a reduced-order homogenized model, linking periodic microscale slip length to an effective macroscopic Navier-slip condition, can be embedded within a real-time feedback controller to stabilize and optimize bulk flow properties by dynamically adjusting actuated surface parameters.
proposals.method: We will derive the homogenized model for a channel flow over a periodic array of actuatable surface elements (e.g., electro-wetted patches). Inline micro-PIV and particle tracking will provide real-time measurements of bulk velocity or particle distribution. A model-predictive control (MPC) algorithm, using the homogenized model as its internal predictor, will compute optimal adjustments to the actuation voltage (modulating local slip) to minimize the error between measured and target flow states.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a foundational framework for the adaptive control of microstructured flows, moving beyond static design. It will demonstrate experimentally the closed-loop stabilization of effective slip, a key transport property. The integration of rigorous multiscale modeling with real-time control offers a new paradigm for smart microfluidic systems.
proposals.novelty: While homogenization theory and flow control are mature fields separately, their integration into a single, real-time experimental feedback loop for microstructured flows is novel. The approach uses microscale actuation to control emergent continuum behavior, a non-obvious strategy distinct from conventional global pressure or flow rate modulation.

proposals.title: Decompression-Driven Bubble Dynamics in High-Viscosity and Viscoelastic Fluids: An Analog Conduit Experiment
proposals.motivation: Bubble formation and transport in high-viscosity fluids are central to processes ranging from volcanic eruptions to polymer devolatilization and biomedical flows. However, predictive models like Stokes' law and Rayleigh-Plesset equations fail for non-Newtonian fluids and high particle (bubble) concentrations, where viscoelastic stresses and collective interactions dominate. A controlled, conduit-scale experimental framework is needed to systematically probe these regimes and validate multiphase flow models.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that decompression-driven bubble nucleation and growth in high-viscosity, viscoelastic fluids will exhibit distinct scaling laws for rise velocity and coalescence, governed by a competition between elastic relaxation timescales, decompression rates, and capillary stresses, leading to anomalous crystalline ordering or clogging transitions analogous to those seen in particulate suspensions.
proposals.method: We will construct a pressurized, vertical column apparatus to perform scaled analog experiments. The conduit will be filled with high-viscosity polydimethylsiloxane (PDMS) and viscoelastic polymer solutions (e.g., polyacrylamide). A controlled pressure drop will induce volatile (air/CO2) exsolution and bubble nucleation. Dynamics will be captured via synchronized high-speed imaging and embedded pressure sensors. Data will be used to validate a 1D conduit-scale numerical model, adapted from volcanology, that couples compressible fluid flow, non-Newtonian rheology (e.g., Oldroyd-B model), and population balance equations for bubble coalescence and growth.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new experimental paradigm for studying multiphase dynamics under decompression, generating benchmark data for bubble growth laws and terminal velocities in viscoelastic fluids. It will directly extend the understanding of transport and exchange in complex fluids, with implications for predicting eruption styles, optimizing polymer processing, and modeling gas transport in biological systems like biofilms or vascular networks.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in adapting the physical modeling framework and solution strategies from volcanology—specifically, the coupled experimental-numerical conduit approach—to the fundamental fluid mechanics of bubbles in synthetic, well-characterized viscoelastic fluids, creating a direct analogy between geophysical and laboratory-scale multiphase flows.

proposals.title: Microscale Transport and Assembly of Particles in Viscoelastic and Yield-Stress Carrier Fluids
proposals.motivation: Microfluidic and porous media studies of particle transport, filtration, and self-assembly predominantly use simple Newtonian oils as the continuous phase, neglecting the complex rheology of many biological and industrial fluids (e.g., mucus, polymer solutions, gels). This gap limits our understanding of how fluid elasticity and yield stress fundamentally alter particle dynamics, clogging, and phase ordering in confined flows, which is critical for applications in targeted drug delivery, biofilm disruption, and material processing.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the viscoelasticity or finite yield stress of a non-Newtonian continuous phase will introduce new regimes of particle transport, dramatically modifying clogging probabilities, enhancing or suppressing anomalous crystalline ordering observed under shear, and enabling novel pathways for the dynamic assembly of soft particle phases.
proposals.method: We will employ microfluidic devices with controlled geometries (constrictions, porous networks) to study the flow of colloidal and microparticle suspensions. The continuous phase will be systematically varied from Newtonian silicone oils to viscoelastic polymer solutions (e.g., polyacrylamide in water-glycerol) and Carbopol gel-like fluids with a yield stress. High-speed microscopy and particle image velocimetry (PIV) will quantify particle trajectories, local shear, and clog formation dynamics. Experiments will be complemented by scaling analyses that incorporate dimensionless numbers (Deborah, Weissenberg, Bingham) to map the transition between flow regimes.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a foundational framework for particle-laden flows in complex carrier fluids, providing predictive rules for clogging and transport in biologically relevant media. It will directly extend the understanding of 'anomalous ordering' in viscoelastic fluids to confined geometries and connect to the dynamics of phase-separated systems like biomolecular condensates, offering new principles for controlling soft material assembly.
proposals.novelty: While particle motion in *viscoelastic* fluids is studied in bulk rheology, its systematic integration into *confined flow* and *porous media* transport problems, with a direct comparison to yield-stress fluids, is novel. This approach challenges the default Newtonian assumption in microfluidic models of particulate systems.

proposals.title: Programmable Droplet Morphodynamics via Lithographically Patterned Wettability Gradients in Microchannels
proposals.motivation: Precise control of droplet shape and position is critical for microfluidic applications in chemical synthesis, diagnostics, and the study of biomolecular condensates. Existing methods for droplet manipulation often rely on active external fields or complex channel geometries, which limit programmability and throughput. A passive, surface-driven approach using engineered wettability patterns could enable new levels of control over droplet dynamics, merging concepts from soft lithography, wetting physics, and multiphase transport.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by lithographically patterning microchannel surfaces with well-defined regions of contrasting wettability and contact line pinning strength, we can deterministically control droplet shape evolution, splitting, and transport in pressure-driven flows, creating a versatile platform for passive droplet morphodynamics.
proposals.method: We will use photolithography and chemical vapor deposition to fabricate PDMS microchannels with spatially patterned surfaces, creating hydrophobic/hydrophilic regions with sharp transitions. High-speed microscopy and micro-PIV will be used to quantify droplet shapes, internal flows, and contact line dynamics in pressure-driven flows. The experimental data will be compared to numerical simulations using a volume-of-fluid (VOF) method with dynamic contact angle models, explicitly incorporating the measured spatially varying contact angles and hysteresis to predict pinning and depinning events.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a direct link between lithographically defined surface energy landscapes and droplet morphodynamics in flow, providing a design framework for passive microfluidic droplet processors. It will yield new fundamental insights into how contact line pinning at engineered defects can be harnessed for fluidic control, with implications for lab-on-a-chip systems and the study of phase-separated systems in confined geometries.
proposals.novelty: While surface patterning for static wetting is known, its systematic application to *dynamically control* droplet shape and fate in a flowing, confined system is novel. This approach integrates Stone group expertise in multiphase transport and biofilm surface interactions with lithographic precision to create a new paradigm for passive microfluidic manipulation.

proposals.title: Flow-Controlled Dissolution of Monodisperse Particles: A Reverse-Engineering Approach to Cargo Release
proposals.motivation: The controlled release of encapsulated agents is critical in drug delivery, agriculture, and materials synthesis. While the formation and stability of monodisperse particles (e.g., via microfluidics) is well-studied, the dissolution process—a reverse problem—is often passive and diffusion-limited. A systematic, physics-based understanding of how flow and particle properties govern the active, spatiotemporal release profile remains a significant gap, limiting the design of next-generation responsive systems.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the dissolution rate and cargo release profile of monodisperse particles can be actively tuned and predicted by coupling external flow fields with the intrinsic viscoelastic properties of the particle matrix, moving beyond passive diffusion.
proposals.method: We will fabricate monodisperse, cargo-loaded hydrogel particles using established microfluidic techniques. A custom microchannel flow cell will impose controlled shear and extensional flows. We will employ high-speed microscopy and micro-PIV to quantify the dissolution front dynamics and the coupled fluid-particle transport. The process will be modeled using a moving boundary problem framework, coupling the Stokes equations for the external flow with a Darcy-type law for solvent ingress and a convection-diffusion equation for cargo release, solved numerically using finite element methods (COMSOL).
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a predictive framework for flow-controlled dissolution, directly linking hydrodynamic conditions to release kinetics. It will provide design principles for 'active' dissolution in applications like targeted drug delivery and reactive material processing, complementing the vast literature on particle formation.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in treating dissolution as an active, flow-driven process to be engineered, rather than a passive end-state, and in rigorously coupling external hydrodynamics with the internal poroelastic transport within the particle—a dual problem rarely addressed with the same precision as formation.

proposals.title: Interfacial Dynamics and Transport in Partially Miscible Fluid-Fluid Systems
proposals.motivation: Classical studies of multiphase flows, from droplet microfluidics to biofilm nutrient exchange, overwhelmingly assume perfectly immiscible fluids. In reality, many biological and industrial fluids (e.g., water-alcohol, polymer solutions) exhibit finite interfacial solubility, enabling dynamic mass transfer that can fundamentally alter interfacial tension, stability, and transport. A systematic framework for these systems is lacking, limiting our understanding of processes like drug delivery from emulsions or solute exchange in condensates.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that finite interfacial mass transfer in partially miscible fluid systems creates a dynamic coupling between interfacial flows and local composition, leading to novel instabilities, altered droplet/bubble dynamics, and non-classical transport regimes that cannot be predicted by immiscible models.
proposals.method: We will employ a combined experimental and theoretical approach. Experimentally, we will use microfluidic flow-focusing and Hele-Shaw cells with well-characterized partially miscible fluid pairs (e.g., water/butanol, PEG/dextran). High-speed interferometry and particle image velocimetry (PIV) with refractive-index-matched tracer particles will quantify concentration fields and flow simultaneously. Theoretically, we will develop a phase-field model coupling the Cahn-Hilliard equation for diffusion-driven phase separation with the Navier-Stokes equations, solved using a finite-element method (COMSOL) to predict interfacial evolution and transport.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new paradigm for analyzing fluid-fluid systems with mass transfer, providing quantitative scaling laws for interfacial deformation and solute flux. It will directly impact the interpretation of transport in biomolecular condensates and offer new strategies for controlling mixing and separation in microfluidic chemical processing.
proposals.novelty: While Stone's group has studied exchange in condensates and transport in biofilms, applying a rigorous fluid dynamical framework to the intrinsically coupled hydrodynamics and interphase diffusion of partially miscible liquids is a novel direction. It moves beyond the static interface approximation ubiquitous in pre-2026 literature.

proposals.title: Dynamics of Particle-Laden Droplets and Biofilms with Surfactant-Mediated Interfacial Transport
proposals.motivation: The assumption of constant interfacial tension is a major simplification in models of multiphase biological and soft matter systems, such as biomolecular condensates, biofilms, and vascular flows. Real interfaces often contain surface-active agents that dynamically adsorb, changing tension and thereby altering transport, stability, and phase behavior. A quantitative, experimental framework to probe these coupled dynamics is lacking, limiting our understanding of processes like nutrient exchange in biofilms or the ripening of particle-stabilized emulsions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that dynamic surfactant adsorption at fluid-fluid and fluid-solid interfaces creates a feedback loop between interfacial tension, Marangoni stresses, and the transport of particles or solutes, leading to non-equilibrium structures and anomalous exchange rates not predicted by constant-tension models.
proposals.method: We will use microfluidic devices to create droplets and thin films with controlled concentrations of synthetic surfactants and colloidal particles or fluorescent tracers. Interfacial tension will be measured in situ via pendant droplet tensiometry and analysis of droplet deformation in flow. Particle and solute transport will be quantified using confocal microscopy and particle image velocimetry (PIV). We will systematically vary surfactant chemistry (e.g., Pluronics, lipids), bulk concentration, and flow shear to map the coupling between adsorption kinetics, tension, and transport, comparing results to a theoretical model incorporating the Langmuir adsorption isotherm and the resulting Marangoni flows.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a foundational experimental and theoretical platform for systems with dynamically evolving interfaces. It will provide quantitative scaling laws for transport in surfactant-laden multiphase systems and reveal new mechanisms for interfacial instability and pattern formation. The findings will directly inform models of complex biological processes, including condensate exchange and biofilm nutrient dynamics.
proposals.novelty: While surfactant effects are well-studied in equilibrium, the non-equilibrium coupling between their adsorption kinetics, interfacial rheology, and the transport of embedded particles or biomolecules in confined flows remains poorly explored. This integrates Stone group expertise in anomalous particle ordering, biofilm exchange, and vascular adaptation into a new, controlled physicochemical context.

proposals.title: Direct Measurement of Slip and Non-Continuum Transport in Nanoscale Gaps with Colloidal Probes
proposals.motivation: The continuum hypothesis underpins classical fluid mechanics but is known to break down in ultra-confined geometries, such as in nanofluidic devices, biological ion channels, and lubrication layers. Pre-2026, experimental validation of flow fields and transport in gaps below ~100 nm remains challenging, limiting our predictive models for mass and momentum transfer in these regimes. This gap in understanding hinders the design of advanced nanotechnological and biomedical systems.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that in rigid nanoscale gaps (10-100 nm), the effective slip length becomes a significant fraction of the gap height, and tracer particle dynamics will reveal non-continuum, non-Brownian transport anomalies due to strong fluid-surface interactions and finite molecular size effects.
proposals.method: We will employ high-speed, dual-angle 3D interferometric particle tracking to monitor the dynamics of fluorescent colloidal probes (50-200 nm diameter) confined between two parallel, atomically smooth surfaces (e.g., silica) with precisely controlled gap heights (10-200 nm). Pressure-driven or electro-osmotic flow will be established. Particle trajectories will be analyzed to reconstruct full 3D flow fields and extract local slip velocities. We will compare results with molecular dynamics simulations (using LAMMPS with SPC/E water models) and kinetic theory predictions for the transition regime.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first direct, three-dimensional experimental mapping of flow fields in rigid nanoconfinement, yielding quantitative slip boundary conditions. It will establish a new experimental framework for testing non-continuum hydrodynamics and inform models for transport in biological nanopores and next-generation membrane technologies.
proposals.novelty: While slip flows have been inferred indirectly, this proposal directly visualizes the breakdown of the no-slip condition and continuum transport using 3D particle tracking at previously inaccessible length scales, merging Stone group expertise in confinement, particle dynamics, and transport.

proposals.title: Mapping the Interplay of Surface Chemistry, Wetting, and Electrokinetics for Programmable Microfluidic Transport
proposals.motivation: Precise control of fluid and particle transport in micro- and nanofluidic systems is critical for lab-on-a-chip diagnostics, membrane separations, and energy applications. While surface chemistry dictates both wetting and electrokinetic phenomena (e.g., electroosmosis, electrophoresis), their coupled effects are often treated independently, leading to suboptimal or unpredictable system performance. A systematic framework to tailor surface chemical characteristics for specific, combined flow functions is lacking.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by independently and combinatorially patterning surface charge density and nanoscale wettability (via grafted polymer brushes or molecular monolayers), we can create a design map that predicts and achieves targeted flow profiles—such as uniform electroosmotic pumps, shear-free particle focusing lanes, or spatially varying mixing zones—in pressure-driven and electric-field-driven flows.
proposals.method: We will fabricate microfluidic channels with spatially defined surface chemistry using photolithography and microcontact printing of silanes and initiators, followed by surface-initiated polymerization to create gradients and patterns of polymer brushes (e.g., PEG, PDMAEMA). Flow functions will be quantified using micro-particle image velocimetry (μ-PIV) and tracer-based fluorescence microscopy under applied pressure and electric fields. The experimental data will be used to validate and refine a coupled computational model solving the Poisson-Boltzmann, Stokes, and continuity equations, incorporating a modified Navier slip boundary condition to account for the measured wettability.
proposals.contribution: This work will yield a quantitative, experimentally validated design principle for coupling surface charge and wettability to engineer complex flow fields. It will directly advance the rational design of next-generation microfluidic devices for targeted drug delivery and separations. Furthermore, it will provide new fundamental insight into the hydrodynamic boundary condition in the presence of simultaneous physicochemical surface forces.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior studies that optimize for a single property (e.g., slip length or zeta potential), this work explicitly maps the non-linear interplay between chemical wettability and electrokinetics to achieve pre-defined, multi-functional flow outcomes. The approach integrates Stone group expertise in interfacial transport, biofilm exchange, and anomalous particle ordering in complex fluids.

proposals.title: Inverse Characterization of Surface Chemistry via Electrokinetic Flow Response in Complex Geometries
proposals.motivation: Surface chemical properties, such as zeta potential and charge heterogeneity, critically govern transport, fouling, and reaction dynamics in microfluidic, biological, and environmental systems. Direct, in-situ measurement of these properties, especially within confined or topologically complex surfaces like porous media or vascular networks, remains a significant challenge. An inverse method that deduces surface chemistry from measurable flow responses would provide a powerful, non-invasive diagnostic tool.
proposals.hypothesis: The spatially resolved electrokinetic flow field generated by an applied electric field in a confined geometry contains a unique signature that can be inverted to quantitatively map the underlying surface charge distribution and chemical identity.
proposals.method: We will fabricate microfluidic channels with well-defined but heterogeneous surface chemistries (e.g., patterned patches of silanes, polymers, or adsorbed biomolecules). Using microparticle image velocimetry (μPIV), we will measure the detailed 2D flow fields induced by alternating current electroosmosis (ACEO) or induced-charge electroosmosis (ICEO). An inverse algorithm will be developed, coupling the Poisson-Nernst-Planck and Stokes equations, and employing an adjoint-based optimization (e.g., limited-memory BFGS) to minimize the discrepancy between simulated and measured flow fields by iteratively updating the boundary condition representing the surface zeta potential distribution.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a generalizable framework for the non-destructive, in-situ characterization of surface chemistry in complex flow systems. It will provide new insights into the coupling between surface heterogeneity and electrokinetic transport, with direct applications in assessing biofilm susceptibility, membrane fouling, and the functional adaptation of vascular and porous networks.
proposals.novelty: While electrokinetics is traditionally used to predict flow from known surface properties, we invert this paradigm to use flow as a diagnostic of the surface. The approach uniquely integrates high-fidelity flow measurement with adjoint-based optimization specifically for the inverse electrokinetic problem in non-trivial geometries.

ideas.title: Adapting Volcanic Conduit Models to Study Bubble Dynamics in High-Viscosity Fluids
ideas.description: This idea adapts solution strategies from volcanology, where the analogous problem of bubble formation and ascent in extremely viscous, often non-Newtonian magma is studied to predict eruption dynamics. The analogous solution involves scaled analog experiments (using silicone oils/syrups) coupled with conduit-scale numerical models that account for decompression-driven volatile exsolution. Adapted to the original research context, this means constructing a pressurized experimental column for high-viscosity liquids (polydimethylsiloxane, polymer solutions) where a controlled pressure drop induces bubble nucleation and growth. The setup will be instrumented with high-speed imaging and pressure sensors to measure bubble dynamics—nucleation rate, growth law, coalescence, and terminal velocity—explicitly probing the failure points of standard scaling laws (e.g., Stokes' law, Rayleigh-Plesset-type models) as viscosity spans several orders of magnitude and fluid rheology transitions to non-Newtonian behavior.

ideas.title: Modeling effective slip from distributed microbubbles using composite material homogenization
ideas.description: This research seed proposes to model flow over surfaces coated with many distributed, nearly flat microbubbles. Drawing an analogy from composite materials science, where effective macroscopic properties are derived from the average behavior of microscopic inclusions, we adapt the solution framework of effective medium theory (EMT). The analogous solution involves using homogenization techniques (e.g., Mori-Tanaka method) to calculate an effective property (like conductivity) from the properties and volume fraction of inclusions embedded in a matrix. Adapted to this context, the surface is treated as a composite slip boundary: the solid areas provide a no-slip condition (matrix), while the nearly flat microbubbles provide a finite local slip length (inclusions). The research idea is to develop a homogenization model to quantify the effective slip length for the overall surface as a function of key parameters: bubble area fraction, their micron-scale geometry (modeled as oblate spheroids), and the intrinsic slip length of a single air-water interface, which may itself be a function of scale and surfactant presence. This provides a predictive, averaged framework for slip quantification without simulating the detailed flow around every individual bubble.

ideas.title: Multi-modal high-speed imaging and sensing for coupled kinematic and dynamic analysis of bubble pinch-off
ideas.description: This redesigned approach simultaneously captures the external neck geometry, the internal liquid velocity field, and the local pressure dynamics during bubble pinch-off. A synchronized system combines ultra-high-speed video microscopy, micro-scale Particle Image Velocimetry (μ-PIV) to map fluid velocities, and a micro-fabricated high-frequency pressure sensor. This integrated dataset allows for the direct calculation of viscous stresses, inertial forces, and pressure gradients governing the rupture. Consequently, the research moves beyond descriptive shape analysis to a quantitative, mechanics-based understanding of the instability and singularity formation.

ideas.title: Mapping the Inertial Regime of Bubbly Flow Phase Space: Control Limits for Size and Fraction at Finite Reynolds Numbers
ideas.description: This research fuses precise multiphase control with inertial fluid dynamics to systematically map the complete, experimentally realizable parameter space for bubble size and volume fraction in flows beyond Stokes regime. It will develop a generalized model and experimental framework to quantify how finite Reynolds number effects—such as wake interactions, modified drag, and bubble deformation—alter the fundamental limits of independent control established for creeping flows. The outcome will be a novel 'phase diagram' for bubbly flows, identifying regions of stable, coupled, or unstable control as a function of Reynolds number, Weber number, and void fraction, which is critical for optimizing high-throughput industrial processes like flotation and chemical reactors.

ideas.title: Use effective medium theory for sparse bubble coverage
ideas.description: This idea adapts perturbation methods from effective medium theory in electromagnetism to the problem of low surface fraction bubble coverage on a wall. In composites, the effective property (e.g., permittivity) of a material with dilute, weakly interacting inclusions is found by perturbing around the host material's solution. Analogously, we treat the classic no-slip flow as the unperturbed (zeroth-order) state. The sparse, no-shear bubbles are treated as a perturbation to the boundary condition. By constructing a perturbation expansion in the small parameter of bubble surface coverage, we can derive an analytical expression for the effective slip length as a first-order correction. This provides a rigorous, predictive framework for quantifying drag reduction from superhydrophobic surfaces or similar sparse bubble arrays, directly linking microstructure to macroscopic flow properties.

ideas.title: Lattice Boltzmann simulation of shear-dependent effective slip over dynamically deforming bubble carpets
ideas.description: This research direction combines the lattice Boltzmann method (LBM) for simulating flow over surfaces with arbitrary, complex bubble shapes with a theoretical framework for shear-dependent effective slip length. The core novelty is using high-resolution LBM simulations to model the dynamic deformation and rearrangement of a 'bubble carpet' under varying shear rates, explicitly calculating the resulting local and effective slip lengths. This creates a closed loop where the simulation outputs (bubble shape, local flow fields) directly inform and validate a constitutive model for effective slip as a function of shear, capturing non-linear effects. The synergy provides a predictive, mechanistic tool to design and optimize superhydrophobic or lubricant-infused surfaces where slip is not a fixed property but a dynamically tunable one governed by micro-scale bubble mechanics.

ideas.title: Scalable, Robust Drag Reduction via Engineered Polydisperse Bubble Layers in Turbulent Pipe Flow
ideas.description: This research investigates a novel method for industrial-scale pipeline drag reduction by intentionally generating and sustaining a polydisperse layer of microbubbles at the pipe wall. Instead of relying on a manufactured, static partial-slip coating, the turbulent flow parameters (e.g., via specific inlet geometries or pulsations) are chaotically controlled to produce a tailored distribution of bubble sizes directly within the working fluid. This dynamic, bubbly layer creates an effective and adaptive slip boundary, where different bubble sizes contribute to stability and coverage across varying shear stresses. The synergy enables a robust, maintainable, and potentially retrofittable system that achieves significant drag reduction without the stringent requirements for monodisperse bubbles or permanent surface engineering.

ideas.title: Homogenization-Based Adaptive Control for Microstructured Flows with Real-Time Feedback
ideas.description: This research direction integrates mathematical homogenization theory with real-time process control to create an adaptive framework for microstructured fluid flows. Homogenization techniques are used to derive a reduced-order model that maps periodic microscale slip boundary conditions to effective macroscopic flow properties. This model is then embedded within a feedback control loop, where inline measurements of bulk flow or particle size are used to dynamically adjust the physical parameters governing the microscale slip (e.g., via actuation of surface properties or geometry), thereby controlling the emergent macroscopic behavior. The synergy creates a closed-loop system that can optimize complex flows not by brute-force manipulation of global inputs, but by intelligently steering their foundational microstructural interactions.

ideas.title: Apply High-Speed Imaging with Laser-Induced Fluorescence to Map Scalar Fields During Bubble Pinch-Off
ideas.description: This idea adapts a high-speed imaging solution from combustion diagnostics—specifically planar laser-induced fluorescence (PLIF)—to the study of bubble pinch-off dynamics. In combustion, PLIF is used to map scalar fields like fuel concentration within turbulent flames. By adapting this method, we propose seeding the liquid phase with a fluorescent dye sensitive to a scalar of interest (e.g., dissolved gas concentration or a chemical tracer). During the rapid pinch-off of a bubble, a high-speed laser sheet would illuminate the neck region, and a synchronized high-speed camera would capture the two-dimensional fluorescence intensity field. This would provide the first direct, time-resolved visualization of the scalar field evolution during pinch-off, revealing details of mixing and mass transfer mechanisms that complement the structural data from traditional high-speed video microscopy.

ideas.title: Closed-Loop Control of Bubble Morphology in Flow via Real-Time Deformation Sensing and Model Predictive Control
ideas.description: This research integrates a dynamic, physics-based model of bubble deformation under shear and pressure with a real-time control architecture. Inline measurement techniques (e.g., high-speed imaging or ultrasound tomography) provide continuous feedback on bubble morphology, not just a scalar size. A model predictive controller uses the deformation model to predict future bubble states and calculates optimal adjustments to flow rates, pressure, or inlet conditions. The goal is to actively steer the multiphase microstructure toward a desired state, enabling precise control over product properties in applications like foam manufacturing, bioreactors, or food processing.

ideas.title: Adapting Implicit Surface Methods from Computer Graphics for Lattice Boltzmann Bubble Modeling
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from computer graphics—the use of implicit surface representations like signed distance fields (SDFs) for modeling deforming fluid interfaces—to the problem of simulating flow over surfaces with arbitrary bubble distributions using the lattice Boltzmann method (LBM). In the analogous field, SDFs robustly handle complex topology changes in fluid animations by defining the surface as an iso-contour of a scalar field. The adapted research idea integrates this SDF representation directly into an LBM framework. Each bubble's shape is defined by an SDF, which is used to apply boundary conditions (e.g., bounce-back) at the fluid-gas interface within the LBM solver. This approach enables the efficient simulation of dynamic, complex bubble populations on surfaces, naturally accommodating merging, splitting, and arbitrary initial geometries without the need for complex explicit surface meshing during the flow simulation.

ideas.title: Adapting Perturbation Methods from Quantum Mechanics to Include Inertial Effects in Microfluidic Models
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption of negligible inertia (Stokes flow) by adapting the perturbation framework from quantum mechanics. The analogous solution uses a known, solvable base system (the unperturbed Hamiltonian/Sokes equations) and treats the complicating factor (external field/inertial terms) as a perturbation expanded in a small parameter. Adapted to fluid dynamics, the Reynolds number (Re) serves as the small parameter. The research would develop a perturbation series solution for low-to-moderate Re flows, where the zeroth-order term is the classic Stokes solution, and successive terms are corrections solved via linear equations forced by lower-order solutions. This provides a rigorous, analytical method to include finite Reynolds number effects in models where Re is small but not zero, extending predictive capability for higher flow rates in microfluidics or porous media.

ideas.title: Apply Homogenization Theory to Derive Effective Acoustic Impedance from Periodically Textured Surfaces
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from acoustics, where homogenization theory derives effective impedance for surfaces with periodic micro-textures, to the problem of periodic slip in fluid/solid mechanics. The analogous acoustics solution replaces a complex microscale geometry with a simple, effective macroscale boundary condition via asymptotic analysis of a periodic unit cell. Adapted here, the method involves applying mathematical homogenization (e.g., two-scale convergence) to the flow equations near a surface with a periodic pattern of slip and no-slip regions. The outcome is a rigorously derived effective boundary condition (like an effective slip tensor) for the macroscopic flow, bypassing the need to resolve the fine-scale pattern in simulations.

ideas.title: Applying Superposition of Eigenfunction Expansions to Periodic Slip in Rectangular Channels
ideas.description: This research seed proposes extending the periodic slip/no-slip analysis to pressure-driven flow in rectangular microchannels by drawing an analogy from heat conduction in composite media. In that field, problems with spatially periodic boundary conditions on rectangular domains are solved using superposition and eigenfunction expansions (e.g., Fourier series). Adapting this solution, we can model the flow by expressing the Stokes stream function or velocity components as a double eigenfunction expansion. One set of eigenfunctions will inherently satisfy the periodicity of the slip condition along the channel length, while the other will satisfy the no-slip conditions on the top, bottom, and side walls of the rectangle. Substituting this expansion into the governing equations and applying orthogonality reduces the problem to solving for the expansion coefficients, providing a semi-analytical framework to quantify the flow rate enhancement and effective slip for non-circular, rectangular geometries.

ideas.title: Mapping the Independent Control Space for Bubble Size and Fraction via Phase-Field Analogy
ideas.description: This research idea adapts the methodology from metallurgy for controlling precipitate microstructure to the precise control of bubbles in a fluid. The analogous problem is the independent control of precipitate size and volume fraction during phase transformations in alloys, which is solved using phase diagrams and kinetic models (like TTT diagrams) to create process maps. The adapted idea is to construct an analogous 'Bubble Process-Property Map' for a given bubble generation system. By systematically varying nucleation triggers (controlling initial bubble number and thus final size) and growth drivers like gas partial pressure or holding time (controlling gas volume transfer and thus fraction), the research will quantify the independent control limits for bubble size and volume fraction across wide parameter ranges, identifying regimes of decoupled and coupled control.

ideas.title: Adapting Astrophysical Dark Matter Halo Models to Predict Nanofluidic Slip from Nanobubble Distributions
ideas.description: This idea applies analogical reasoning from astrophysics to nanofluidics. The original concept of scaling down to model nanobubble distributions on chips to predict slip parallels the cosmological challenge of mapping dark matter distribution from its gravitational effects. The analogous solution uses statistical halo models to connect observable phenomena to unseen mass distributions. Adapted here, we develop a statistical 'nanobubble halo model' that treats surface nanobubbles as a correlated population on the chip surface. By using sparse, experimentally accessible measurements of slip or flow resistance in a nanofluidic device as the observable output, the model inversely infers the most probable nanobubble distribution (size, density, arrangement) responsible. This allows for predicting device-scale slip and transport characteristics based on manufacturing or surface treatment parameters, without requiring exhaustive nanoscale imaging of every bubble.

ideas.title: Apply Schwarz-Christoffel mapping for Stokes flow in microfluidic channels with complex geometry
ideas.description: This idea expands the original seed by drawing an analogy from electrostatics in integrated circuit design. The analogous problem is calculating the capacitance of irregular, polygonal conductor shapes, which is solved using Schwarz-Christoffel conformal mapping to transform the geometry to a domain where Laplace's equation is tractable. We adapt this solution to 2D Stokes flow, governed by a biharmonic equation for the stream function. The research will develop a method to apply Schwarz-Christoffel mapping to transform the complex, often polygonal, cross-sections of microfluidic channels (featuring no-slip walls, inlets, outlets, and possibly symmetry axes) into a canonical domain like a half-plane or an infinite strip. In this transformed plane, the Stokes flow problem with mixed boundaries becomes simpler to solve, either analytically or numerically. The solution can then be mapped back to the physical plane to obtain the flow field, enabling the analysis and design of microfluidic devices with non-standard, fabrication-relevant geometries.

ideas.title: Acousto-optical hybrid trap for dynamic bubble lattice manipulation
ideas.description: This redesigned system overcomes the force limitation of pure optical tweezers by using a synchronized acoustic field to provide the primary force for arresting bubble motion within a flowing lattice. A high-speed imaging and feedback system identifies target bubbles and triggers focused acoustic pulses to create temporary, stable trapping zones. Once a bubble is acoustically captured and its flow-relative velocity is near zero, optical tweezers are applied for sub-micrometer precision rearrangement. This hybrid approach enables real-time, selective manipulation of individual bubbles within dynamic, flowing lattices that were previously inaccessible to optical forces alone.

ideas.title: Mapping and active navigation of the coupled bubble size-volume fraction state space
ideas.description: This research quantifies the fundamental coupling between bubble size and volume fraction by mapping the entire accessible state space for a given fluid and forcing system. Instead of seeking independent control, it develops a predictive model of the coupled dynamics and a closed-loop control framework to actively navigate to desired, physically achievable combinations of the two parameters. The outcome is a comprehensive understanding of the trade-offs and hard limits of controllability, enabling robust and predictable operation within the feasible envelope.

ideas.title: Constrained Multi-Objective Optimization for Robust Microfluidic Bubble Generator Design
ideas.description: This research develops a robust design system for microfluidic bubble generators that overcomes the ill-posed nature of the pure inverse problem. It uses a physics-informed data-driven model to learn the forward relationship between device parameters and bubble outcomes. The core of the approach is a constrained optimization solver that, given a target bubble size and production rate, identifies all feasible design points that satisfy secondary objectives like stability, energy efficiency, and manufacturability. The output is not a single solution but a Pareto front of optimal trade-offs, enabling a designer to select the most suitable and robust configuration for a specific application.

ideas.title: Active stabilization of dynamic slip interfaces for robust industrial drag reduction
ideas.description: This project investigates a closed-loop, active system for drag reduction in large-scale pipelines that overcomes the inherent instability of passive gas layers. The core approach combines engineered surfaces with embedded micro-sensors and actuators to monitor local shear and pressure, triggering on-demand micro-gas injection or surface energy modulation to maintain a stable slip boundary condition. Research will focus on control algorithms that minimize energy input for gas replenishment while maximizing net energy savings from drag reduction across variable flow regimes. The outcome aims to be a scalable, fault-tolerant technology that delivers reliable performance in the non-ideal, fluctuating environment of industrial fluid transport.

ideas.title: Adaptive Pulsatile Gas Delivery via Cardiovascular Hemodynamic Principles
ideas.description: This idea challenges the fundamental assumption of constant gas pressure in chemical processes by drawing an analogy from cardiovascular hemodynamics. In the human circulatory system, blood is delivered via pulsatile pressure, not a constant pressure, which is dynamically regulated by feedback mechanisms to optimize delivery efficiency. The adapted research idea proposes to design a gas delivery system that mimics this principle. A controller would use real-time process data (similar to baroreceptor feedback) to vary gas pressure in a pulsatile or otherwise non-steady pattern. This adaptive pulsatile feed could enhance mixing, improve interfacial mass transfer in gas-liquid or gas-solid reactions, prevent channeling in packed beds, and potentially lead to more efficient and selective chemical processes by synchronizing reactant delivery with the kinetic demands of the system.

ideas.title: Adaptive Piezoelectric Gas Pulsation Using Bio-Inspired Valving
ideas.description: This idea expands the original concept of using piezoelectric actuators for gas pressure pulsation by drawing an analogy from microfluidic pumping solutions in biomedical engineering. The analogous solution uses piezoelectric actuation combined with passive, bio-inspired check valves to create precise, unidirectional fluid flow. Adapted to the gas pressure context, the research idea involves designing a system where a piezoelectric actuator, similar to those in inkjet printheads, is integrated with a network of passive one-way valves surrounding a gas compression chamber. The actuator provides high-frequency mechanical pulsation, while the valve array ensures the gas pressure waves are rectified into a controlled, directional pulsation, enabling efficient high-frequency gas pressure modulation for applications in fluidics, mixing, or propulsion.

ideas.title: Modeling stress field interference between multiple no-slip patches using acoustic shielding principles
ideas.description: This research seed expands the original idea of studying interactions between multiple slip domains by drawing an analogy from acoustics and noise control. In that field, the total sound radiation from multiple sources or around multiple barriers is solved by considering wave interference and mutual shielding, where the pressure field of one element alters the effective environment for others. Adapting this solution, we propose to model each closely spaced no-shear stress region (slip domain) as a source of a perturbed elastic stress field. The study will develop a framework to superpose these fields, accounting for how the stress perturbation from one domain modifies the local driving stress at the location of adjacent domains. This interaction, analogous to acoustic shielding, will lead to a non-additive influence on the total effective slip, where domains can either shield or amplify each other's effect depending on their spacing and orientation. The goal is to derive scaling laws for the total effective slip as a function of domain density and spatial configuration.

ideas.title: Quantify weak inertial corrections via matched asymptotics in a defined Re regime
ideas.description: This research precisely defines the scope of 'finite Reynolds number' by focusing on the weakly inertial regime (e.g., 0.1 < Re < 20) where systematic corrections to Stokes flow can be derived. It employs the method of matched asymptotic expansions to develop a first- or second-order inertial correction model, ensuring mathematical consistency with the Stokes limit. The approach is validated by comparing predictions for canonical problems (like flow past a sphere) against high-fidelity numerical simulations and experimental data, providing a rigorously benchmarked extension of the model.

ideas.title: Adaptive Flow Control via Inline Particle Size Measurement, Inspired by Adaptive Optics
ideas.description: This idea adapts the real-time measurement and correction framework from adaptive optics in astronomy to chemical process control. In adaptive optics, a wavefront sensor measures distortions and a deformable mirror corrects them at high speed. The analogous solution is implemented here by using an inline particle size analyzer as the 'sensor' and a fast flow control actuator as the 'corrector.' This creates a closed-loop system that continuously monitors product particle size (e.g., in crystallization, milling, or precipitation) and dynamically adjusts process flow rates to maintain a precise target size distribution, significantly improving product consistency and yield compared to traditional batch or open-loop methods.

ideas.title: Adapting Robotic Swarm Reconfiguration Algorithms for Optical Tweezer-Based Lattice Editing
ideas.description: This idea expands the original concept of using optical tweezers for lattice manipulation by drawing an analogy from multi-robotic systems. In swarm robotics, centralized algorithms efficiently reconfigure robot swarms by planning collision-free paths. We adapt this solution by developing a control system for an optical tweezer array that treats individual bubbles in a flowing lattice as simple agents. The system will use a graph-based planner to calculate simultaneous, optimal optical trapping paths to rearrange multiple bubbles from a disordered or initial lattice state into a precise, pre-defined target lattice configuration, thereby enabling rapid, programmable editing of the flowing bubble lattice structure.

ideas.title: Analyze effective slip in pulsatile flow using frequency-domain methods from electrical circuit analysis
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that effective slip is a steady-state property by analyzing it under time-dependent pressure gradients. Drawing an analogy from electrical circuit theory, where AC circuit analysis uses frequency-domain transforms to solve for time-varying voltages and currents, the research adapts this solution. The adapted idea is to analyze effective slip in pulsatile flow by representing the unsteady pressure gradient in the frequency domain (e.g., as a Fourier series) and solving the linearized flow equations for each harmonic component. This allows the effective slip length to be characterized as a complex, frequency-dependent quantity, revealing how slip performance amplifies or attenuates at different pulsation frequencies and how the slip response lags behind the driving pressure gradient.

ideas.title: Active Stability Control via Adaptive Gas Pressure Modulation
ideas.description: This research develops a closed-loop control system that replaces the constant gas pressure assumption with an actively modulated pressure stream designed to enhance flow stability. The system uses high-speed imaging or laser diagnostics to monitor a real-time stability signature, such as the growth rate of interfacial waves. An adaptive feedback controller then calculates and applies specific pressure perturbations to dampen these instabilities as they emerge. The goal is to achieve a superior, more repeatable flow state by making the system actively self-correcting against inherent disturbances.

ideas.title: Fault-Tolerant Hybrid Control Using Soft Sensors for Robust Real-Time Process Adjustment
ideas.description: This research designs a robust process control system that overcomes the vulnerability of single-sensor feedback. It integrates multiple inline analytical probes with a dynamic process model to create a validated, virtual 'soft sensor' for the primary quality attribute, such as particle size. The control architecture continuously performs sensor diagnostics and can seamlessly switch to a model-predictive or rule-based fallback strategy upon detecting sensor anomalies, maintaining stable operation and product quality despite hardware failures or measurement drift.
